```mermaid
    hotJ[Hot Junction<br/>T_H] --> tcWire1[Type K<br/>Thermocouple Wire A]
    tcWire1 --> coldJ[Isothermal Block<br/>Cold Junction T_CJ]
    coldJ --> tcWire2[Type K<br/>Thermocouple Wire B]
    tcWire2 --> tcTerm[Terminal<br/>Block]
    tcTerm --> ampStage[Instrumentation<br/>Amplifier]
    ampStage --> adc1[ADC Channel 1<br/>Raw E_measured]

    coldJ --> cjcSensor[Semiconductor<br/>Temp Sensor LM35]
    cjcSensor --> adc2[ADC Channel 2<br/>T_CJ reading]

    adc1 --> procBlock[Microcontroller<br/>or DSP]
    adc2 --> procBlock
    procBlock --> lkupTable[NIST ITS-90<br/>Polynomial Lookup]
    lkupTable --> displayOut[Display<br/>T_H in deg C]
```

> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow:**
> Hot Junction + Cold Junction (with reference sensor) → Two ADC channels → Software combines via NIST polynomials → Final $T_H$ displayed.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1: Define a charge amplifier and state why it is preferred over a voltage amplifier for piezoelectric sensors. (3 marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar Model]`

**Model Answer:**
A charge amplifier is an active circuit using an operational amplifier with a **capacitor in the feedback path**, that converts the high-impedance charge output of a piezoelectric sensor into a low-impedance voltage output.

It is preferred over a voltage amplifier because:
1. The output voltage is **independent of the cable capacitance** between sensor and amplifier, depending only on the feedback capacitor $C_f$ which is a stable, on-board component.
2. It **eliminates triboelectric noise** and cable-flexing artifacts, since the cable voltage is forced to zero by the virtual ground.
3. The transfer function is $V_{out} = -Q_{in}/C_f$, providing predictable and adjustable sensitivity by changing $C_f$.

---

### Question 2: What is the function of a Phase Sensitive Detector (PSD)? Mention one application. (3 marks)
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Similar Model]`

**Model Answer:**
A Phase Sensitive Detector (PSD) is a synchronous demodulator that recovers the **amplitude and phase** of a small AC signal of known frequency, even when it is buried in noise. It multiplies the input signal with a reference signal at the same frequency, and low-pass filters the product to extract the in-phase DC component $V_{out} = (1/2) V_s V_r \cos\phi$.

**Application:** It is the heart of a **lock-in amplifier**, used in optical spectroscopy, atomic force microscopy, and any measurement of a small AC signal immersed in a noisy environment.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a charge amplifier. Derive the expression for its output voltage and mention the role of the feedback resistor. (7 marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Description:**
A charge amplifier is built around an op-amp with a **capacitor $C_f$ in the feedback path**, and (in practice) a **resistor $R_f$ in parallel with $C_f$** to provide a DC path. The inverting input is the "virtual ground."

**Step 2 — Operation:**
The piezoelectric sensor is modeled as a charge source $Q$ in parallel with its own capacitance $C_s$. Connecting cables add $C_c$. Because the inverting node is held at virtual ground (0 V), the cable capacitance has no voltage across it and **does not store any of the signal charge**. Hence, *all* charge is routed into $C_f$.

**Step 3 — Derivation:**
By charge conservation at the inverting node (assuming no current into the op-amp itself):
$$Q_{in} = C_f \cdot V_{out}$$
$$\boxed{V_{out} = -\frac{Q_{in}}{C_f}}$$

The negative sign reflects the inverting configuration.

**Step 4 — Role of $R_f$:**
Without $R_f$, the input bias current of the op-amp would slowly charge $C_f$ until the output saturates. $R_f$ provides a DC discharge path. However, $R_f$ and $C_f$ together form a high-pass filter with cutoff:
$$f_{-3dB} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}$$
For low-frequency measurements (e.g., seismic), $R_f$ must be very large (GΩ range).

**Step 5 — Numerical Illustration:**
For a charge-output accelerometer of sensitivity 100 pC/g and $C_f = 1$ nF:
$$V_{out}/g = \frac{100 \times 10^{-12}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.1\,\text{V}/g = 100\,\text{mV}/g$$

#### Part (b) — Explain the principle of operation of a Phase Sensitive Detector. Derive the output expression and explain how it rejects noise. (7 marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:**
A PSD multiplies the input signal $v_s(t) = V_s \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ with a clean reference $v_r(t) = V_r \sin(\omega t)$, and then low-pass filters the product.

**Step 2 — Multiplication:**
$$v_p(t) = V_s V_r \sin(\omega t + \phi) \sin(\omega t) = \frac{V_s V_r}{2}[\cos\phi - \cos(2\omega t + \phi)]$$

**Step 3 — Low-Pass Filtering:**
After removing the $2\omega$ term:
$$\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{V_s V_r}{2}\cos\phi}$$

**Step 4 — Noise Rejection:**
For a noise component at frequency $\omega_n \ne \omega$:
$$v_n(t)\cdot v_r(t) = V_n V_r \sin(\omega_n t)\sin(\omega t)$$
Its average over a complete period is **zero** by orthogonality of sinusoids. So all out-of-band noise averages to zero, while the in-phase signal survives.

**Step 5 — Quadrature Extension:**
Using a second PSD with reference shifted by 90°, we get $X = V_s\cos\phi$ and $Y = V_s\sin\phi$, from which:
$$V_s = \sqrt{X^2 + Y^2}, \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}(Y/X)$$
This recovers **both amplitude and phase** uniquely.

**Step 6 — Application:**
Lock-in amplifiers using PSDs can detect signals as small as **nanovolts** in millivolts of noise — a capability unattainable by ordinary filters.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Explain the principle of True-RMS conversion using the log-antilog method. Compare it with the rectifier-type AC meter. (7 marks)
`[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Adapted]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of True RMS:**
$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt}$$

**Step 2 — Log-Antilog Method Sequence:**
1. Square the input: $v^2(t)$.
2. Take logarithm: $\log v^2(t) = 2\log v(t)$.
3. Average over a period: $\frac{1}{T}\int \log v^2(t)\,dt$.
4. Multiply by 2 and take antilog.
5. Take square root to obtain $V_{rms}$.

**Step 3 — Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.3.)

**Step 4 — Comparison Table:**

| Feature | True RMS | Rectifier-Type (Average) |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $\sqrt{\langle v^2 \rangle}$ | $1.11 \cdot V_{avg}$ (sine only) |
| Accuracy for sine | Exact | Exact (with 1.11 factor) |
| Accuracy for square | Exact | 11% low |
| Accuracy for SCR-chopped | Exact | Up to 50% low |
| Cost / Complexity | Higher (special IC) | Lower (diodes + meter) |
| Crest-Factor Handling | Up to 5 or more | Limited (≈3) |

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
True-RMS converters are *waveshape-independent* and essential for non-sinusoidal waveforms (SCR/triac-controlled heater voltages, PWM motor drives, harmonic-rich industrial loads).

#### Part (b) — Design a thermocouple temperature measurement system with cold junction compensation. Explain the function of each block. (7 marks)
`[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Adapted]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sensor Selection:**
Use a **Type K thermocouple** (Chromel–Alumel) for a measurement range of 0 °C to 1200 °C. Seebeck coefficient $S_K \approx 41\,\mu V/°C$ at 25 °C.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.6.)

**Step 3 — Block-by-Block Function:**

- **Hot Junction ($T_H$):** The measuring point, where the two dissimilar metals are welded.
- **Thermocouple Wires (A and B):** Carry the millivolt-level signal from the hot to the cold junction.
- **Cold Junction (Isothermal Block at $T_{CJ}$):** The terminals of the thermocouple, held at a temperature measured by a **semiconductor temperature sensor** (LM35, AD590, or DS18B20). This is the *reference* temperature.
- **Semiconductor Reference Sensor (LM35):** Produces 10 mV/°C, providing an analog voltage proportional to $T_{CJ}$.
- **Instrumentation Amplifier (e.g., AD620):** Amplifies the small (µV) thermocouple voltage to a level suitable for the ADC. Provides common-mode rejection.
- **ADC:** Two channels — one for $E_{measured}$, one for $T_{CJ}$.
- **Microcontroller/DSP:** Computes $E_{corrected} = E_{measured} + S_K \cdot T_{CJ}$ (the CJC correction), then applies NIST ITS-90 polynomial coefficients to linearize the Seebeck curve and produce $T_H$ in °C.
- **Display:** Shows the final hot-junction temperature.

**Step 4 — Mathematical Verification:**
If the measured voltage is $E_{measured} = S_K(T_H - T_{CJ})$, and the correction $\Delta E = S_K \cdot T_{CJ}$ is added, then:
$$E_{corrected} = S_K(T_H - T_{CJ}) + S_K \cdot T_{CJ} = S_K \cdot T_H$$
$$T_H = \frac{E_{corrected}}{S_K}$$

**Step 5 — Practical Tips:**
- Use **shielded, twisted-pair** thermocouple wire to reject EMI.
- The isothermal block must be **thermally uniform** to avoid thermal gradients.
- Software should apply **cold-junction compensation at every ADC sample**, not just once.
- The **NIST polynomial** for Type K uses 8th-order or 12th-order coefficients, with separate equations for different sub-ranges.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Charge Amplifier Sign Error:** Forgetting the negative sign in $V_{out} = -Q_{in}/C_f$ is the most common 1-mark loss. The op-amp is *inverting*, hence the negative sign.
> 2. **PSD Output Factor:** Many students write $V_{out} = V_s \cos\phi$ without the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ factor. State explicitly: with reference $V_r = 2$ V (normalized), the factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ cancels; otherwise retain it.
> 3. **True RMS vs Sine RMS:** Saying "$V_{rms} = V_{peak}/\sqrt{2}$" without qualifying *"for a pure sine wave only"* will lose the *application* mark. The whole point of True-RMS is being *waveshape-independent*.
> 4. **Ratiometric Logic:** Students often think the bridge output *and* the ADC reference should be supplied from *different* sources to avoid coupling. It is the **opposite** — both must come from the *same* source for ratiometric cancellation to work.
> 5. **Log-Amp Polarity:** A log-amp using NPN transistor in feedback gives **negative** output for positive input. The sign matters when cascading with antilog blocks for RMS computation.
> 6. **Thermocouple CJC Direction:** The correction voltage must be **added** to the measured EMF (not subtracted), because the cold junction produces an *opposing* effect that must be cancelled.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **charge amplifier** converts $Q_{in}$ to $V_{out}$ using a feedback capacitor; output depends *only* on $C_f$, not on cable capacitance.
- A **feedback resistor $R_f$** in parallel with $C_f$ prevents op-amp saturation but introduces a low-frequency cutoff $f_{-3dB} = 1/(2\pi R_f C_f)$.
- **Impedance converters** include V–I, I–V, V–V (buffer), and I–I types; the most common is the transimpedance amplifier (TIA) for photodiodes.
- A **PSD** multiplies the input by a reference and low-pass filters the result; output is $V_s \cos\phi$. Quadrature PSDs recover both amplitude and phase.
- The PSD can reject noise at all frequencies **except** the reference frequency — making it the most selective filter ever built (Q values in the $10^8$ range).
- **True RMS** computes $\sqrt{\langle v^2(t) \rangle}$ regardless of waveshape; the log-antilog method is a classical implementation.
- **Ratiometric conversion** ties the ADC reference to the sensor excitation; any drift in the excitation cancels exactly in the digital output.
- A **logarithmic amplifier** uses the BJT's exponential $I$-$V$ characteristic in feedback; output is $V_{out} = -V_T \ln(V_{in}/R_{in}I_S)$.
- A **thermocouple** generates a Seebeck voltage proportional to $(T_H - T_{CJ})$ — a *difference*, not an absolute temperature.
- **Cold Junction Compensation (CJC)** adds a synthesized voltage equal to $S_{AB} \cdot T_{CJ}$ to the measured EMF, so the resulting voltage represents $T_H$ only.
- Modern CJC uses a **semiconductor sensor** (LM35, AD590) at the terminal block; software linearizes using **NIST ITS-90 polynomial coefficients**.
- The **isothermal terminal block** is a critical design element — any thermal gradient across it causes CJC errors.
- For ratiometric measurement, both the **bridge excitation** and the **ADC reference** must be derived from the *same* voltage source.
- The **KVL/KCL** charge conservation principle is the foundation of charge amplifier analysis; the op-amp's **virtual ground** is the key to understanding the cable-capacitance immunity.
- For exam questions, always state the **negative sign** in charge-amp output, the **$\tfrac{1}{2}$ factor** in PSD output, and the **unit conversion** (µV → mV) for thermocouple problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linearization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT602 - INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linearization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Linearization** is the process of mathematically or electronically converting a non-linear input–output relationship of a transducer, sensor, or measurement system into a linear one, so that the output becomes a directly proportional function of the input within a specified operating range. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCICT602), linearization is treated as a fundamental *signal-conditioning* operation performed either through **hardware linearization** (analog circuits) or **software linearization** (digital algorithms).

Mathematically, a non-linear system characterized by

$$y = f(x)$$

is linearized into

$$y \approx a_0 + a_1 x$$

where $a_0$ and $a_1$ are constants determined by the chosen linearization technique.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Linearization falls under *Static Characteristics & Compensation* of an instrumentation system. It is a direct application of the linearity specification of an instrument (Module 1, CO1: *Understand the static and dynamic characteristics of measurement systems*).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Crooked Ruler"

Imagine you are an engineer trying to measure the length of a metal rod with a ruler that is slightly **bent** in the middle. If you simply read the markings, your measurements will be wrong near the bend. There are two ways to fix this:

1. **Physically straighten the ruler** (Hardware Linearization — using analog circuits to reshape the transfer function).
2. **Write a correction table** that says, "Whenever the ruler reads 7 cm at the bend, the true length is 6.8 cm" (Software Linearization — using polynomials or lookup tables in a microcontroller or computer).

In both cases, the *output becomes directly proportional* to the true input. That correction process is **linearization**.

### 1.3 Why is Linearization Required?

Most real-world transducers exhibit some degree of non-linearity due to underlying physics:

| Transducer | Source of Non-Linearity |
|------------|--------------------------|
| Thermocouple | Non-linear Seebeck coefficient vs. temperature |
| Thermistor (NTC) | Exponential resistance–temperature relationship |
| RTD (PT100) | Quadratic resistance–temperature curve |
| Strain Gauge (bridge) | Slight non-linearity in Wheatstone bridge output |
| LVDT | Slight second-order deviation at stroke extremes |
| Orifice Plate | Square-root relationship of flow vs. differential pressure |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU examiner often asks: *"Why cannot we simply replace a non-linear sensor with a linear one?"* The answer lies in cost, physical constraints, and the inherent physics — linearization allows the *use* of robust, cheap, or physically ideal sensors while still achieving linear readout.

### 1.4 Types of Linearization — A Taxonomy

Linearization techniques in instrumentation are classified into **two broad families**:

1. **Hardware (Analog) Linearization**
   - Piecewise linear approximation using diodes, Zeners, and op-amps
   - Log/antilog amplifiers (e.g., for thermistor linearization)
   - Multiplier/Divider ICs (e.g., AD534) for square-root extraction
   - Function-fitting analog circuits

2. **Software (Digital) Linearization**
   - Lookup Table (LUT) method
   - Piecewise linear interpolation
   - Polynomial curve fitting (least-squares based)
   - Spline interpolation
   - Artificial Neural Network (ANN) based linearization (advanced)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Framework — The "Why" and "How"

Linearization is built on a sequence of logical operations. The KTU model answer for any linearization question should reflect this ordered thought process.

**Step 1 — Identify the non-linearity:**
Acquire calibration data $(x_i, y_i)$ from the transducer over its operating range.

**Step 2 — Choose a linearization strategy:**
- If only a few calibration points exist → use **piecewise linear** approximation.
- If many calibration points exist with smooth non-linearity → use **polynomial curve fitting**.
- If real-time correction is needed with limited memory → use a small **LUT**.

**Step 3 — Compute the linearization parameters:**
For polynomial fitting, solve the **normal equations** derived from the least-squares criterion.

**Step 4 — Apply the linearization equation:**
For each new raw reading $x$, compute the corrected output $\hat{y}$ using the linearizing equation.

**Step 5 — Validate with error metrics:**
Compute the **maximum non-linearity error**, **RMS error**, or **% linearity** to validate fit.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** A complete linearization answer must include (a) the data/model, (b) the chosen technique, (c) the derivation of parameters, and (d) an error analysis. Skipping (d) often costs 2–3 marks.

### 2.2 Hardware Linearization — The Square-Root Extractor

A classic KTU question is linearizing an **orifice plate** flow meter, where

$$Q = k \sqrt{\Delta P}$$

To obtain $Q$ linearly proportional to a measurement, a **square-root extractor** is built using op-amps and multiplier ICs. The linearization is achieved by the analog equation

$$V_{out} = \sqrt{\frac{V_{in}}{K}}$$

implemented using log-antilog amplifier stages.

### 2.3 Software Linearization — Lookup Table Method

A **Lookup Table (LUT)** stores calibrated output values $y_i$ at discrete input points $x_i$. For an arbitrary reading $x$ falling between two table entries $x_i$ and $x_{i+1}$, **linear interpolation** is applied:

$$\hat{y} = y_i + \frac{(y_{i+1} - y_i)}{(x_{i+1} - x_i)} \cdot (x - x_i)$$

This is computationally cheap and widely used in microcontroller-based systems.

### 2.4 Polynomial Linearization — General Model

A non-linear transfer function is approximated by a polynomial of degree $n$:

$$\hat{y} = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + a_3 x^3 + \ldots + a_n x^n$$

In matrix form:

$$\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X} \mathbf{a}$$

where
- $\mathbf{y} = [y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_N]^T$
- $\mathbf{a} = [a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_n]^T$
- $\mathbf{X}$ is the **Vandermonde matrix** with entries $X_{ij} = x_i^{j}$.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Equation** | **Description / Use** | **Units / Variables** |
|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| $y = a_0 + a_1 x$ | Best-fit straight line | $a_0$ = intercept, $a_1$ = slope |
| $y = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2$ | Second-order polynomial fit | $a_2$ = curvature coefficient |
| $a_1 = \frac{N \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{N \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$ | Least-squares slope (linear fit) | $N$ = number of data points |
| $a_0 = \bar{y} - a_1 \bar{x}$ | Least-squares intercept | $\bar{x}, \bar{y}$ = means |
| $\mathbf{a} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$ | Polynomial fit in matrix form | General least-squares solution |
| $\varepsilon_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ | Residual error at point $i$ | In output units |
| $E_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \varepsilon_i^2}$ | Root-mean-square error | In output units |
| $E_{max} = \max \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Maximum deviation (use `\vert`) | In output units |
| $\% \text{Linearity} = \frac{E_{max}}{\text{Full Scale Output}} \times 100$ | Percentage linearity | Dimensionless |
| $R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | Coefficient of determination | $0 \le R^2 \le 1$ |
| $\hat{y} = y_i + \frac{(y_{i+1} - y_i)}{(x_{i+1} - x_i)}(x - x_i)$ | Piecewise linear interpolation | Between adjacent table entries |
| $Q = k \sqrt{\Delta P}$ | Orifice flow equation (to be linearized) | $Q$ = flow, $\Delta P$ = pressure diff |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Linearization is indispensable in:

- **Industrial process control:** DCS/PLC systems assume linear PV-to-control signal mapping.
- **Medical instrumentation:** Pulse oximeters, ECG amplifiers need linear output for diagnostics.
- **Aerospace:** Flight control sensors (pressure transducers, gyros) require linear readout for stability analysis.
- **Automotive:** Lambda sensors (oxygen sensors) use linearization in ECU firmware.
- **IoT/Embedded Systems:** Sensor fusion and digital twin models assume near-linear sensor response.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Best-Fit Straight Line Using Least Squares

**Problem Setup:**
We are given $N$ calibration data points $(x_i, y_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$ from a non-linear transducer. We wish to find the straight line

$$\hat{y} = a_0 + a_1 x$$

that best fits the data in the **least-squares sense**, i.e., minimizing the sum of squared residuals

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i - a_0 - a_1 x_i \right)^2$$

**Step 1 — Write the objective function.**
The sum of squared residuals is

$$S(a_0, a_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i - a_0 - a_1 x_i \right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Take partial derivatives with respect to $a_0$ and $a_1$.**

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial a_0} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - a_0 - a_1 x_i) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial a_1} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i (y_i - a_0 - a_1 x_i) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the first equation.**

$$\sum y_i - N a_0 - a_1 \sum x_i = 0$$

$$N a_0 = \sum y_i - a_1 \sum x_i$$

$$a_0 = \frac{\sum y_i}{N} - a_1 \frac{\sum x_i}{N} = \bar{y} - a_1 \bar{x}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the second equation.**

$$\sum x_i y_i - a_0 \sum x_i - a_1 \sum x_i^2 = 0$$

Substitute $a_0 = \bar{y} - a_1 \bar{x}$:

$$\sum x_i y_i - (\bar{y} - a_1 \bar{x}) \sum x_i - a_1 \sum x_i^2 = 0$$

$$\sum x_i y_i - \bar{y} \sum x_i + a_1 \bar{x} \sum x_i - a_1 \sum x_i^2 = 0$$

Recall that $\bar{x} \sum x_i = \bar{x} \cdot N \bar{x} = N \bar{x}^2$. Group the $a_1$ terms:

$$a_1 \left( N \bar{x}^2 - \sum x_i^2 \right) = \bar{y} \sum x_i - \sum x_i y_i$$

Using the identity $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = \sum x_i^2 - N \bar{x}^2$ and $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) = \sum x_i y_i - N \bar{x} \bar{y}$:

$$a_1 = \frac{N \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{N \sum x_i^2 - \left( \sum x_i \right)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{a_1 = \frac{N \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{N \sum x_i^2 - \left( \sum x_i \right)^2}, \quad a_0 = \bar{y} - a_1 \bar{x}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — General Polynomial Fit (Normal Equations)

For an $n$-th degree polynomial $\hat{y} = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \ldots + a_n x^n$, minimizing

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i - \sum_{j=0}^{n} a_j x_i^j \right)^2$$

with respect to each $a_k$ gives the **normal equation**:

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial a_k} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^k \left( y_i - \sum_{j=0}^{n} a_j x_i^j \right) = 0$$

which yields the system $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \mathbf{a} = \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$, with solution:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{a} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example — Linearization of a Thermocouple

**Problem (typical 14-mark KTU question):**
The following calibration data is obtained for a Type-K thermocouple used in a temperature measurement system:

| $x_i$ (°C) | 0 | 100 | 200 | 300 | 400 | 500 |
|-------------|---|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| $y_i$ (mV) | 0 | 4.10 | 8.13 | 12.21 | 16.40 | 20.65 |

(a) Find the best-fit straight line $\hat{y} = a_0 + a_1 x$ using least squares.
(b) Calculate the maximum non-linearity error and the percentage linearity.

**Step 1 — Compute the required summations.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 6 \\
\sum x_i &= 0 + 100 + 200 + 300 + 400 + 500 = 1500 \\
\sum y_i &= 0 + 4.10 + 8.13 + 12.21 + 16.40 + 20.65 = 61.49 \\
\sum x_i^2 &= 0 + 10{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 90{,}000 + 160{,}000 + 250{,}000 = 550{,}000 \\
\sum x_i y_i &= 0 + 410 + 1626 + 3663 + 6560 + 10{,}325 = 22{,}584
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the slope $a_1$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_1 &= \frac{N \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{N \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2} \\
&= \frac{6 \times 22{,}584 - 1500 \times 61.49}{6 \times 550{,}000 - (1500)^2} \\
&= \frac{135{,}504 - 92{,}235}{3{,}300{,}000 - 2{,}250{,}000} \\
&= \frac{43{,}269}{1{,}050{,}000} \\
&= 0.04121 \;\; \text{mV/°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the intercept $a_0$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{1500}{6} = 250 \\
\bar{y} &= \frac{61.49}{6} = 10.248 \\
a_0 &= \bar{y} - a_1 \bar{x} = 10.248 - 0.04121 \times 250 = 10.248 - 10.3025 = -0.0545 \;\; \text{mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Best-fit line equation.**

$$\boxed{\hat{y} = -0.0545 + 0.04121 \cdot x \;\; \text{(mV, with } x \text{ in °C)}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute residuals and maximum error.**

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ (mV) | $\hat{y}_i$ (mV) | $\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ (mV) |
|-------|------------|------------------|--------------------------------|
| 0     | 0.00       | $-0.0545$        | 0.0545 |
| 100   | 4.10       | 4.0665           | 0.0335 |
| 200   | 8.13       | 8.1875           | 0.0575 |
| 300   | 12.21      | 12.3085          | 0.0985 |
| 400   | 16.40      | 16.4295          | 0.0295 |
| 500   | 20.65      | 20.5505          | 0.0995 |

$$E_{max} = 0.0995 \;\; \text{mV (occurs at } x = 500 \text{ °C)}$$

**Step 6 — Percentage linearity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FSO} &= y(500) - y(0) = 20.65 - 0 = 20.65 \;\; \text{mV} \\
\% \text{Linearity} &= \frac{E_{max}}{\text{FSO}} \times 100 = \frac{0.0995}{20.65} \times 100 \approx 0.482\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion.**
The percentage linearity of approximately **0.48 %** indicates a good linear fit, and the calibrated thermocouple output can be considered acceptably linear over 0–500 °C.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Observation:** Always state the operating range explicitly in the conclusion. Saying "the system is linear" without specifying the range costs 1 mark.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Methods

| **Method** | **Accuracy** | **Computational Cost** | **Memory Required** | **KTU Use Case** |
|------------|--------------|------------------------|---------------------|------------------|
| Lookup Table (LUT) | High (if dense) | Very Low | High (large tables) | Microcontroller-based sensors |
| Piecewise Linear Interpolation | Medium | Low | Medium | Real-time embedded systems |
| Polynomial (Least Squares) | High | Medium (matrix solve) | Low | Offline calibration |
| Spline Interpolation | Very High | High | Medium | High-precision lab instruments |
| Analog (Op-amp) Linearization | Medium | None (real-time) | None | Legacy industrial systems |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Linearization System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Quantity] --> B[Sensor / Transducer]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    C --> D[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    D --> E[Microcontroller / Processor]
    E --> F[Linearization Algorithm]
    F --> G[Calibration Memory LUT or Coefficients]
    G --> F
    F --> H[Linearized Digital Output]
    H --> I[Display / DCS / PLC]
    F --> J[Error Analysis and Validation]
    J --> K[Calibration Certificate Output]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow for Software Linearization (Polynomial Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B[Collect N Calibration Data Points]
    B --> C[Choose Polynomial Degree n]
    C --> D[Build Vandermonde Matrix X]
    D --> E[Compute X transpose times X]
    E --> F[Compute X transpose times y]
    F --> G[Invert Matrix X transpose X]
    G --> H[Solve for Coefficient Vector a]
    H --> I[Validate Using R squared or E max]
    I --> J{Error within Tolerance?}
    J -->|No| K[Increase Polynomial Degree or Use Spline]
    K --> C
    J -->|Yes| L[Store Coefficients in Memory]
    L --> M[End Deploy to System]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Hardware Linearization of Thermistor (Log-Antilog Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Thermistor Resistance R_T] --> B[Wheatstone Bridge]
    B --> C[Differential Amplifier]
    C --> D[Log Amplifier]
    D --> E[Inverting Amplifier with Temperature Dependent Gain]
    E --> F[Antilog Amplifier]
    F --> G[Linearized Voltage Output V out]
    G --> H[Display or ADC]
```

### 4.4 Comparison Diagram — Non-Linear vs. Linearized Response

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input x] --> B[Non-linear Transducer f x]
    B --> C[Raw Output y raw]
    C --> D[Linearization Block y = a0 + a1 x]
    D --> E[Linearized Output y linear]
    F[Best Fit Straight Line] -.Reference.-> D
    G[Calibration Data Points] -.Used to compute a0, a1.-> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> In Mermaid, the rectangular blocks above represent conceptual signal flow, not physical circuit elements. For circuit-level schematics (e.g., op-amp pinouts), refer to the manufacturer's datasheet — the KTU examiner does not expect circuit diagrams, only **functional block diagrams**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define linearization. Why is it required in instrumentation systems?*

**Model Answer:**
Linearization is the process of converting a non-linear input–output relationship of a transducer into a linear one, either by hardware (analog circuits) or software (mathematical algorithms) means. It is required because (a) most transducers are inherently non-linear due to underlying physical laws, (b) downstream instrumentation like displays, controllers, and DCS assume a linear input–output mapping, and (c) linear relationships simplify calibration, error analysis, and control loop tuning. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Differentiate between hardware and software linearization. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Hardware Linearization | Software Linearization |
|--------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Implementation | Analog circuits (op-amps, multipliers, log amplifiers) | Digital algorithms (LUT, polynomial, spline) |
| Real-time | Yes, inherently real-time | Requires ADC + processor |
| Flexibility | Low (fixed by circuit) | High (changeable coefficients) |
| Example: | Square-root extractor for orifice flowmeter | Polynomial fit for thermocouple in microcontroller |
| Example 2: | Log-antilog amplifier for thermistor | Lookup table for RTD |

Hardware linearization uses **op-amp-based analog circuits** (e.g., a square-root extractor using AD534 multiplier for orifice flow), while software linearization uses **digital algorithms** (e.g., a 3rd-order polynomial fit stored in firmware for a Type-K thermocouple). **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*

**(a)** Explain the method of **least squares linearization** to obtain the best-fit straight line for a set of calibration data. Derive the expressions for the slope and intercept. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The calibration data of a thermistor used in a temperature measurement system is given below:

| $x_i$ (°C) | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 60 | 70 |
|-------------|----|----|----|----|----|----|
| $R_T$ (kΩ) | 12.10 | 8.31 | 5.92 | 4.34 | 3.27 | 2.51 |

Determine the best-fit straight line $\hat{R}_T = a_0 + a_1 x$ using the least-squares method and compute the maximum non-linearity error. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
*(Proceed as in Section 3.1 above. Briefly, define the objective function $S = \sum (y_i - a_0 - a_1 x_i)^2$, take partial derivatives, set to zero, and derive:)*

$$a_1 = \frac{N \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{N \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}, \quad a_0 = \bar{y} - a_1 \bar{x}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Summations.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 6 \\
\sum x_i &= 20 + 30 + 40 + 50 + 60 + 70 = 270 \\
\sum y_i &= 12.10 + 8.31 + 5.92 + 4.34 + 3.27 + 2.51 = 36.45 \\
\sum x_i^2 &= 400 + 900 + 1600 + 2500 + 3600 + 4900 = 13{,}900 \\
\sum x_i y_i &= (20)(12.10) + (30)(8.31) + (40)(5.92) + (50)(4.34) + (60)(3.27) + (70)(2.51) \\
&= 242 + 249.3 + 236.8 + 217 + 196.2 + 175.7 = 1317.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Slope.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_1 &= \frac{6 \times 1317.0 - 270 \times 36.45}{6 \times 13900 - 270^2} \\
&= \frac{7902 - 9841.5}{83400 - 72900} \\
&= \frac{-1939.5}{10500} = -0.1847 \;\; \text{kΩ/°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Intercept.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= 45, \quad \bar{y} = 6.075 \\
a_0 &= 6.075 - (-0.1847)(45) = 6.075 + 8.3115 = 14.3865 \;\; \text{kΩ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Best-fit line.**

$$\hat{R}_T = 14.387 - 0.1847 \, x \;\; \text{kΩ}$$

**Step 5 — Residuals.**

| $x_i$ | $R_{T,i}$ | $\hat{R}_T$ | $\vert \varepsilon \vert$ |
|-------|-----------|--------------|--------------------------|
| 20    | 12.10     | 10.693       | 1.407 |
| 30    | 8.31      | 8.846        | 0.536 |
| 40    | 5.92      | 6.999        | 1.079 |
| 50    | 4.34      | 5.152        | 0.812 |
| 60    | 3.27      | 3.305        | 0.035 |
| 70    | 2.51      | 1.458        | 1.052 |

$$E_{max} = 1.407 \;\; \text{kΩ (at } x = 20 \text{°C)}$$

This high error indicates a straight line is a poor fit — a second-order polynomial or log transformation is more appropriate for a thermistor (which is exponential). However, the linearization still provides a usable first-order approximation over a limited range.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Explain with a block diagram the **hardware linearization of an orifice plate flowmeter** using a square-root extractor. Derive the relationship used for linearization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **software linearization** of a non-linear transducer using a **lookup table (LUT)** and **piecewise linear interpolation**. What are the advantages and limitations of this method? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
An orifice plate produces a differential pressure $\Delta P$ proportional to the square of the flow rate $Q$:

$$Q = k \sqrt{\Delta P}$$

To obtain a voltage $V_{out}$ proportional to $Q$, we pass $V_{in} \propto \Delta P$ through a **square-root extractor** built using **log and antilog amplifiers** (or a multiplier IC in feedback configuration). The signal flow is:

$$\Delta P \rightarrow \text{Log Amp} \rightarrow \text{Divide by 2} \rightarrow \text{Antilog Amp} \rightarrow V_{out} \propto Q$$

Mathematically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in} &\propto \Delta P \\
V_1 &= K_1 \log(V_{in}) \\
V_2 &= \tfrac{1}{2} V_1 \\
V_{out} &= K_2 \cdot 10^{V_2} = K_2 \cdot 10^{(K_1/2) \log V_{in}} = K_2 \cdot V_{in}^{K_1/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

By choosing $K_1/2 = 1/2$, we obtain $V_{out} \propto \sqrt{V_{in}} \propto Q$, achieving linearization. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (b):**
A Lookup Table stores pairs $(x_i, y_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$ covering the operating range. For a new reading $x$ between two adjacent table entries $x_k \le x \le x_{k+1}$, the linearized output is

$$\hat{y} = y_k + \frac{(y_{k+1} - y_k)}{(x_{k+1} - x_k)} (x - x_k)$$

The block diagram consists of: (1) Transducer, (2) ADC, (3) Address decoder to find $k$, (4) Interpolation engine, (5) Linearized output.

**Advantages:** Simple, fast, no matrix computation, low microcontroller overhead.
**Limitations:** Memory intensive for fine resolution; accuracy depends on table density; not smooth (piecewise linear). **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Linearization Questions (where students lose marks):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to specify units** in the final equation (e.g., "mV" or "kΩ"). Always carry units throughout.
> 2. **Confusing $R^2$ with % linearity** — $R^2$ is a statistical fit metric, while % linearity is a deviation metric relative to FSO.
> 3. **Skipping the error analysis step** — KTU awards 2–3 marks specifically for calculating $E_{max}$ and % linearity. Never end your answer with just the equation.
> 4. **Using the wrong sign convention** in the least-squares derivation — ensure the residual is $y_i - \hat{y}_i$, not $\hat{y}_i - y_i$.
> 5. **In hardware linearization**, students often draw block diagrams without explaining the mathematical basis (log/antilog derivation). Always pair the diagram with the corresponding equation.
> 6. **Not stating the operating range** in the conclusion — "the system is linear" is incomplete; say "the system is linear over 0–500 °C with 0.48 % non-linearity."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Linearization** = correction of non-linear transducer response to obtain linear output.
- **Need:** Most transducers (thermocouples, thermistors, orifice plates) are inherently non-linear; downstream systems require linear inputs.
- **Two main families:** Hardware (analog circuits) and Software (digital algorithms).
- **Hardware techniques:** Square-root extractors, log/antilog amplifiers, multiplier-based function generators.
- **Software techniques:** Lookup Table, Piecewise Linear Interpolation, Polynomial (Least Squares) Fit, Splines.
- **Key formula (least-squares slope):**
$$a_1 = \frac{N \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{N \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$$
- **Key formula (intercept):** $a_0 = \bar{y} - a_1 \bar{x}$
- **General polynomial matrix form:** $\mathbf{a} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$
- **Error metrics to always report:** $E_{max}$, $E_{rms}$, and % linearity relative to FSO.
- **% Linearity formula:** $\%\text{Linearity} = \frac{E_{max}}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$
- **Typical exam data sets:** 5–7 calibration points, straight line or 2nd-order polynomial.
- **Conclusion template:** "The transducer is linear over [range] with [X] % non-linearity, which is acceptable for [application]."
- **Diagrams expected:** Functional block diagram of linearization system + flow chart of algorithm.
- **CO mapping:** CO1 (Understand static/dynamic characteristics); CO2 (Apply linearization to instrumentation design).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

Design of Capacitance Based Level Transmitter for both Conducting and Non-Conducting Liquids - Signal Conditioning Circuit for pH Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT602 - INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Amplification and Conversion of its Output to 4 to 20 mA Current. Design of RTD with Lead Wire Compensation and Conversion of its Output to 4 to 20 mA Current. Design of Capacitance Based Level Transmitter for both Conducting and Non-Conducting Liquids - Signal Conditioning Circuit for pH Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Signal Amplification and 4–20 mA Current Conversion

### Formal Definition
**Signal amplification** in the context of industrial instrumentation refers to the process of magnifying the low-level analog output of a primary sensing element (e.g., thermocouple, RTD, pH electrode, strain gauge) to a usable voltage or current level that can drive downstream indicators, controllers, or recorders. **Current loop transmission (4–20 mA)** is the standardized current signaling technique wherein the measured process variable is linearly represented as a direct current ranging from **4 mA** (representing the **Lower Range Value, LRV**, i.e., 0% of span) to **20 mA** (representing the **Upper Range Value, URV**, i.e., 100% of span).

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine whispering a secret across a large noisy hall. The original whisper (sensor's millivolt-level signal) is so weak that listeners at the back cannot hear it. An amplifier acts like a microphone-and-speaker system that boosts the whisper to a room-filling voice. Now imagine you have to send this message through a long, noisy wire to another building (a control room). Voltage signals get distorted because of wire resistance picking up electrical noise. So instead, you convert the message into "water pressure" (current) inside a closed pipe. As long as the pipe is intact, the pressure at the far end is exactly what was sent — the wire length and resistance no longer matter. The 4 mA "live zero" is like leaving a small background pressure (instead of 0) so that you can immediately detect a broken pipe (which would read 0 mA, signaling an **open-loop fault**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight — 4–20 mA is the *de facto* industrial standard (ANSI/ISA-50.1)** because: (1) it is a *current-mode* signal, hence immune to voltage drop over long cable runs; (2) the **4 mA live zero** enables *open-loop fault detection*; (3) it intrinsically provides **loop-powered** operation where the same two wires carry both power and signal; (4) it is intrinsically safe for hazardous (Ex) areas because energy is limited.

### Current-to-Voltage Transfer Relationship
For a process variable $x$ lying between its minimum $x_{min}$ and maximum $x_{max}$:

$$I_{out} = 4\,\text{mA} + \left( \frac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}} \right) \times 16\,\text{mA}$$

The corresponding percentage of span is:

$$\%\,\text{span} = \frac{I_{out} - 4}{20 - 4} \times 100 = \frac{I_{out} - 4}{16} \times 100$$

---

## 1.2 RTD with Lead Wire Compensation

### Formal Definition
A **Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)** is a primary temperature sensor whose resistance $R_T$ changes predictably with temperature. The most common industrial RTD is the **Pt100** (Platinum, 100 Ω nominal resistance at 0 °C) described by the **Callendar-Van Dusen equation**. **Lead wire compensation** refers to the technique used to nullify the parasitic resistance introduced by the connecting wires between the RTD element and the measuring instrument, which would otherwise cause a positive, temperature-dependent measurement error.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a Pt100 RTD as a long, thin straw whose electrical resistance tells you the temperature. Now, to connect this straw to a measuring device in the control room, you must attach extension leads (wires). These wires have their own resistance that adds to the straw's resistance, like dirt sticking to the straw. A **2-wire RTD connection** is like measuring the straw *with the dirt still on it* — you over-estimate the resistance. A **3-wire connection** is like using a clever balancing scale (Wheatstone bridge) that subtracts the dirt on the way out. A **4-wire connection** is like using two separate clean probes that inject current and measure voltage independently — the dirt doesn't matter at all because no current flows through the voltage-sensing leads.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Standard Callendar-Van Dusen Constants for Pt100 (IEC 60751):**
> $R_0 = 100.00\ \Omega$ at $T = 0\,^\circ\text{C}$
> $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$
> $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-2}$
> $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-4}$ (used for $T < 0\,^\circ\text{C}$ only)

For $T \ge 0\,^\circ\text{C}$, the simpler linear approximation is frequently used:
$R_T \approx R_0\,(1 + \alpha T)$, where $\alpha \approx 0.00385\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$.

---

## 1.3 Capacitance-Based Level Transmitter

### Formal Definition
A **capacitance-based level transmitter** measures the level of a liquid (or granular solid) inside a vessel by detecting the change in electrical **capacitance** between two electrodes (or one electrode and the grounded tank wall) as the dielectric medium between them changes with the rising/falling level. It is a non-contact (for non-conducting liquids with an insulated probe) or contact-type (for conducting liquids using the liquid itself as one plate) primary level sensor.

### Intuitive Analogy
Picture a long, vertical metal rod (the probe) suspended inside a water tank. The water is the second "plate" of a capacitor; air is another. Water has a **dielectric constant** $\varepsilon_r \approx 80$ (very high), while air has $\varepsilon_r \approx 1$. So the *effective* dielectric between the rod and the tank wall is a mixture of air and water. As the water rises, more of the gap is filled with water, the capacitance rises, and the level is determined. Think of it as a sponge between two parallel plates — as the sponge absorbs water, the system's "electrical storage capacity" increases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Two Operational Modes:**
> 1. **Non-Conducting Liquids** (e.g., oil, solvents, deionized water): the probe is bare metal; the liquid is a *dielectric* displacing air. $C$ rises as level rises.
> 2. **Conducting Liquids** (e.g., water, acids, brine): the probe is *insulated* (e.g., PTFE-coated); the conducting liquid acts as the *outer plate*; the insulation is the *dielectric*. $C$ rises as level rises *because the effective area of the outer plate increases* even though the dielectric thickness is constant.

---

## 1.4 Signal Conditioning Circuit for pH Measurement

### Formal Definition
A **pH electrode** is a high-impedance electrochemical sensor (typical source resistance $10^{8}$ to $10^{9}\ \Omega$) that develops a Nernstian potential proportional to the hydrogen-ion activity. The signal conditioning circuit must perform **(a) impedance matching** using a high-input-impedance instrumentation amplifier (typically $> 10^{12}\ \Omega$), **(b) signal amplification** of the small Nernst voltage (≈ 59.16 mV/pH unit at 25 °C), and **(c) reference electrode buffering** to drive the 4–20 mA current loop.

### Intuitive Analogy
A pH probe is like a very quiet, very "leaky" person. They whisper important information (millivolts) but won't say it twice, and if you try to measure their voice using normal equipment, the very act of measuring changes what they say (loading error). You need a *super-sensitive listener* (instrumentation amplifier with FET inputs) standing right at their ear, amplifying the whisper, and then shouting it down a robust communication line (4–20 mA current loop) to the control room, where it can be heard by anyone, anywhere.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Nernst Equation for pH:**
> $E = E_0 - S \cdot (\text{pH} - \text{pH}_0)$, where the theoretical **slope** $S = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{F} = 59.16\ \text{mV/pH}$ at 25 °C, $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$, $F = 96485\ \text{C/mol}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Building Blocks of an Industrial Transmitter

Regardless of the primary sensor, every modern 4–20 mA "smart" transmitter contains the following cascaded functional blocks:

1. **Primary Sensor** (RTD, pH probe, capacitive probe, etc.) — converts the measurand into a passive electrical quantity (resistance, voltage, capacitance).
2. **Signal Conditioning Front-End** — bridges the sensor to electronic circuitry (impedance conversion, excitation, lead-wire compensation).
3. **Analog Signal Processing** — amplification, linearization, cold-junction compensation, filtering.
4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — quantizes the conditioned analog signal.
5. **Microcontroller / DSP** — applies digital linearization (e.g., Callendar-Van Dusen inversion), range scaling, damping.
6. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) + V-to-I Converter** — produces the standardized 4–20 mA loop current.
7. **Power Supply / Loop-Power Interface** — extracts operating power from the same 2-wire loop.

---

## 2.2 The 4–20 mA Current Loop — Detailed Theory

### 2.2.1 Why Current, Not Voltage?
A voltage signal $V_s$ transmitted through a long cable of total loop resistance $R_{wire}$ produces a received voltage:

$$V_{rx} = V_s - I_{loop}\,R_{wire}$$

If the load is purely voltage-sensing (infinite input impedance), no current flows and $V_{rx} = V_s$. But in real circuits, leakage and load currents cause a drop. In *current* signaling:

$$I_{loop} = \text{constant (set by the transmitter)},\quad V_{rx} = I_{loop}\,R_{wire} \rightarrow \text{drop is "absorbed" by the loop supply}$$

The receiver senses current (by measuring the voltage across a precision *sense resistor* $R_s$), not voltage, so the indication is independent of wire length, wire gauge, and contact resistance — *as long as the loop supply voltage is high enough to overcome the sum of all voltage drops*.

### 2.2.2 Loop Compliance Voltage
The maximum permissible total loop resistance at 20 mA is governed by:

$$R_{loop,\,max} = \frac{V_{supply} - V_{tx,\,min}}{20\,\text{mA}}$$

For example, with a 24 V DC supply and a transmitter that needs 12 V minimum for operation:
$R_{loop,\,max} = (24 - 12) / 0.020 = 600\ \Omega$.

### 2.2.3 Voltage-to-Current (V/I) Conversion Circuit
The classic op-amp based V/I converter (also called a *Howland current pump* or *improved Howland topology*):

$$I_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_{sense}} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1}$$

The output is a current independent of the load impedance, set entirely by the input voltage and four precision resistors.

---

## 2.3 RTD — Theoretical Analysis

### 2.3.1 Callendar-Van Dusen Equation (Full)
For $-200\,^\circ\text{C} \le T \le 0\,^\circ\text{C}$:
$$R_T = R_0\left[1 + AT + BT^2 + C(T - 100)\,T^3\right]$$

For $0\,^\circ\text{C} \le T \le 850\,^\circ\text{C}$:
$$R_T = R_0\left[1 + AT + BT^2\right]$$

The coefficients are physically tied to platinum's temperature coefficient of resistance and higher-order curvature corrections.

### 2.3.2 Three Lead-Wire Compensation Schemes

| Configuration | Effective Measured Resistance | Lead-Wire Error Contribution | Cost / Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **2-wire** | $R_T + 2R_{lead}$ | Full additive error, uncompensated | Low cost, lowest accuracy |
| **3-wire** (Wheatstone bridge) | $R_T + R_{lead} - R_{lead} = R_T$ (ideal case) | One lead cancels the other if balanced | Moderate cost, good accuracy |
| **4-wire** (Kelvin) | $R_T$ (independent of lead resistance) | Zero (current and voltage leads are separate) | High cost, highest accuracy |

### 2.3.3 Wheatstone Bridge for 3-Wire RTD
A bridge with arms $R_1, R_2, R_3$ (all equal to $R_0$ at 0 °C), and the RTD in the fourth arm with two identical lead resistances $r$. The bridge output voltage is:

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R_0 + r + \Delta R/2} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R_0}\quad \text{when } r \to 0$$

The two lead resistances appear in *adjacent* arms of the bridge and are subtracted from each other, eliminating their effect to first order.

---

## 2.4 Capacitance-Based Level Transmitter — Theory

### 2.4.1 Governing Capacitance Equation
For a coaxial cylindrical probe of inner radius $a$, outer radius $b$, and length $L$ immersed to a height $h$ in a non-conducting liquid of dielectric constant $\varepsilon_{liq}$ (air above the liquid with $\varepsilon_{air} \approx 1$):

$$C_{total} = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(b/a)}\left[\varepsilon_{air}(L - h) + \varepsilon_{liq}\,h\right] = C_{air} + \left(\frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0(\varepsilon_{liq} - 1)}{\ln(b/a)}\right)h$$

The capacitance is *linear* in the liquid height $h$, with a baseline $C_{air}$ (empty tank) and slope proportional to $(\varepsilon_{liq} - 1)$.

### 2.4.2 Conducting Liquid Mode
When the liquid is conductive, the *insulated* probe has constant capacitance per unit length (since the dielectric is the fixed insulation layer). The variable part is the *active length*:

$$C = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_{ins}\,h}{\ln(b/a)}$$

The instrument therefore reads $h$ directly, with $C$ proportional to $h$ and passing through the origin ($C = 0$ when $h = 0$).

### 2.4.3 Signal Conditioning
A typical capacitance-to-voltage converter uses an **AC bridge** excited by a high-frequency sine wave (typically 10 kHz to 1 MHz) and a synchronous demodulator (phase-sensitive detector) to recover the capacitance variation as a slowly varying DC voltage, which is then scaled and converted to 4–20 mA.

---

## 2.5 pH Signal Conditioning — Theory

### 2.5.1 Nernst Equation
$$E = E^0 - \frac{2.303\,RT}{F}\,(\text{pH} - \text{pH}_{iso})$$

At 25 °C, the slope is exactly $-59.16$ mV/pH. The **isopotential pH** $\text{pH}_{iso}$ is the pH at which the electrode potential is independent of temperature (typically $\text{pH}_{iso} = 7.0$, $E^0 = 0$ mV).

### 2.5.2 Instrumentation Amplifier (INA) Front-End
The standard 3-amplifier INA provides:
- Differential gain: $G = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}$
- Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR): typically $> 100$ dB at DC
- Input impedance: $> 10^{12}\ \Omega$ (FET inputs)
- Bias current: $< 10$ pA

The output is then offset (to map 0 pH → 4 mA) and scaled (to map 14 pH → 20 mA) by the V/I converter.

---

## KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Topic | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4–20 mA conversion | $I_{out} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}$ | mA |
| 2 | Loop compliance | $R_{loop,\,max} = \dfrac{V_{supply} - V_{tx,\,min}}{20\,\text{mA}}$ | Ω |
| 3 | Pt100 resistance (T ≥ 0) | $R_T = R_0(1 + AT + BT^2)$ | Ω, T in °C |
| 4 | Linear Pt100 approx. | $R_T \approx R_0(1 + \alpha T)$ | $\alpha = 0.00385\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ |
| 5 | 3-wire bridge output | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R_0 + r}$ | $r$ = lead resistance |
| 6 | Coaxial capacitance (level) | $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r L}{\ln(b/a)}$ | F |
| 7 | Capacitive level (non-conducting) | $C = C_0 + \Delta C \cdot h$ | linear in $h$ |
| 8 | Nernst slope (pH) | $S = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{F} = 59.16$ mV/pH at 25 °C | |
| 9 | INA differential gain | $G = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | V-to-I converter | $I_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_{sense}}$ (improved Howland) | mA |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: 4–20 mA Mapping Equation

**Problem Setup.** A process variable $x$ lies in the range $[x_{min}, x_{max}]$. The current loop must output 4 mA when $x = x_{min}$ and 20 mA when $x = x_{max}$. Find $I_{out}(x)$.

### Derivation
The mapping is linear, so we write the equation of a straight line through the two endpoint pairs $(x_{min}, 4)$ and $(x_{max}, 20)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{out} - 4 &= \frac{20 - 4}{x_{max} - x_{min}} \cdot (x - x_{min}) \\
I_{out} - 4 &= \frac{16}{x_{max} - x_{min}} \cdot (x - x_{min}) \\
I_{out} &= 4 + \frac{16\,(x - x_{min})}{x_{max} - x_{min}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the *universal* KTU-level formula for mapping any physical quantity to a 4–20 mA current. Units of $I_{out}$ are mA; the span $x_{max} - x_{min}$ must match the units of $x$.

### Worked Numerical Example
A temperature transmitter has range 0 °C to 200 °C. Find the current output for $T = 75\,^\circ\text{C}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{out} &= 4 + \frac{16\,(75 - 0)}{200 - 0} \\
&= 4 + \frac{16 \times 75}{200} \\
&= 4 + \frac{1200}{200} \\
&= 4 + 6.0 \\
&= 10.0\,\text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

Check: 75 °C is 37.5 % of span; 4 + 16 × 0.375 = 4 + 6 = 10 mA. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Pt100 Resistance at 150 °C (Linear vs. Quadratic)

**Problem Setup.** Compute the resistance of a Pt100 at 150 °C using (a) the linear approximation and (b) the full Callendar-Van Dusen quadratic, then find the percentage error.

### Step 1 — Linear Approximation
With $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$, $\alpha = 0.00385\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$:
$$R_T = 100\,(1 + 0.00385 \times 150) = 100\,(1 + 0.5775) = 157.75\ \Omega$$

### Step 2 — Quadratic Callendar-Van Dusen
With $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$ and $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_T &= 100\,\bigl[1 + 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \times 150 + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7}) \times (150)^2\bigr] \\
&= 100\,\bigl[1 + 0.586245 + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7}) \times 22500\bigr] \\
&= 100\,\bigl[1 + 0.586245 - 0.01299375\bigr] \\
&= 100 \times 1.57325125 \\
&= 157.325\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Error
$$\%\text{error} = \frac{157.75 - 157.325}{157.325} \times 100\% = 0.270\%$$

This is a non-negligible error at 150 °C — proves why quadratic linearization (or digital lookup) is required for accurate KTU-grade instrumentation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Wheatstone Bridge Output for 3-Wire RTD

**Problem Setup.** A 3-wire RTD is connected to a Wheatstone bridge with all four arms nominally $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ and excitation $V_{ex}$. The two lead wires each have resistance $r$, and the RTD itself has resistance $R_T = R_0 + \Delta R$. Show that the bridge output is independent of $r$ to first order.

### Schematic Assignment
- Arm AB: $R_1 = R_0$ (fixed)
- Arm BC: $R_2 = R_0$ (fixed)
- Arm CD: RTD $R_T$ with one lead of resistance $r$ in series
- Arm DA: $R_3 = R_0$ in series with the other lead of resistance $r$

### Standard Bridge Equation
The output voltage is:
$$V_o = V_{ex}\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_{CD}}{R_{DA} + R_{CD}}\right)$$

Substituting:
$$V_o = V_{ex}\left(\frac{R_0}{2R_0} - \frac{R_T + r}{R_0 + r + R_T + r}\right)$$

Wait — this is a 2-wire *r-in-series* model. The 3-wire trick requires the bridge to be drawn with one lead in *each* adjacent arm. Reassignment:

- Arm AB: $R_0$ (reference)
- Arm BC: $R_0 + r$ (lead + reference)
- Arm CD: $R_T$ (RTD)
- Arm DA: $R_0 + r$ (lead + reference)

Then:
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o &= V_{ex}\left(\frac{R_0 + r}{R_0 + R_0 + r} - \frac{R_T}{R_T + R_0 + r}\right) \\
&= V_{ex}\left(\frac{R_0 + r}{2R_0 + r} - \frac{R_T}{R_T + R_0 + r}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For a *balanced* bridge ($R_T = R_0$):
$$V_o = V_{ex}\left(\frac{R_0 + r}{2R_0 + r} - \frac{R_0}{2R_0 + r}\right) = 0$$

The bridge remains balanced regardless of $r$. This is the key insight of the 3-wire technique. For small $\Delta R$, Taylor-expanding:

$$V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R_0 + r/2}$$

So the lead resistance $r$ causes a *gain error* of order $r/2R_0$ but does *not* introduce an offset, and the gain error is small for $r \ll 2R_0$ (e.g., $r = 1\ \Omega$ introduces a 0.5% gain error).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Capacitance-Based Level — Coaxial Probe Equation

**Problem Setup.** A coaxial cylindrical probe of inner conductor radius $a = 2\,\text{mm}$, outer shield radius $b = 5\,\text{mm}$, and total length $L = 1.0\,\text{m}$ is immersed in oil ($\varepsilon_{liq} = 2.2$) to a height $h = 0.6\,\text{m}$. Compute the total capacitance (oil + air column).

### Step 1 — Capacitance per Unit Length
For a coaxial geometry filled with dielectric $\varepsilon_r$:
$$C' = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}{\ln(b/a)}\ \text{F/m}$$

### Step 2 — Air and Oil Sections
$$C'_{air} = \frac{2\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.0}{\ln(5/2)} = \frac{5.563 \times 10^{-11}}{0.9163} = 6.071 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F/m}$$

$$C'_{oil} = \frac{2\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.2}{\ln(5/2)} = 1.336 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F/m}$$

### Step 3 — Total Capacitance
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total} &= C'_{air}\,(L - h) + C'_{oil}\,h \\
&= 6.071 \times 10^{-11} \times 0.4 + 1.336 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.6 \\
&= 2.428 \times 10^{-11} + 8.014 \times 10^{-11} \\
&= 1.044 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F} \\
&\approx 104.4\,\text{pF}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Empty-Tank (Baseline) Capacitance
For an empty tank ($h = 0$):
$$C_{empty} = C'_{air}\,L = 6.071 \times 10^{-11} \times 1.0 = 60.71\,\text{pF}$$

### Step 5 — Slope $\Delta C / h$
$$\frac{\Delta C}{h} = C'_{oil} - C'_{air} = 1.336 \times 10^{-10} - 6.071 \times 10^{-11} = 7.29 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F/m} = 72.9\,\text{pF/m}$$

This is the calibration constant the transmitter will use to convert capacitance back to level.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: pH Measurement Range to 4–20 mA

**Problem Setup.** A pH transmitter uses an instrumentation amplifier with gain $G = 10$ to amplify the Nernst voltage. The pH range is 0 to 14. The amplified voltage is fed to a V/I converter. Design the scaling and offset such that pH 0 → 4 mA and pH 14 → 20 mA.

### Step 1 — Nernst Voltage at 25 °C
$$E(\text{pH}) = -59.16\,\text{mV} \cdot \text{pH}\quad (\text{assuming } E^0 = 0,\ \text{pH}_{iso} = 7)$$

| pH | $E$ (mV) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0 |
| 7 | −414.12 |
| 14 | −828.24 |

### Step 2 — Amplified Voltage ($G = 10$)
$V_{amp}(\text{pH}) = -0.5916 \cdot \text{pH}\ \text{V}$

- pH 0: $V_{amp} = 0$ V
- pH 14: $V_{amp} = -8.282$ V

### Step 3 — Polarity Inversion
A typical V/I converter expects a *positive* input that grows with the measurand. Insert an inverter of gain $-1$ (or flip pH-axis convention):
$$V_{in} = +0.5916 \cdot \text{pH}\ \text{V}$$

- pH 0: $V_{in} = 0$ V
- pH 14: $V_{in} = 8.282$ V

### Step 4 — Scaling to 4–20 mA
We need $I_{out} = 4$ mA at $V_{in} = 0$ V and $I_{out} = 20$ mA at $V_{in} = 8.282$ V. Use a precision V/I converter with **zero adjustment** and **span adjustment**:

$$I_{out} = 4\,\text{mA} + \frac{16\,\text{mA}}{8.282\,\text{V}} \cdot V_{in} = 4 + 1.932\,V_{in}\ \text{mA}$$

Check:
- pH 0 ($V_{in} = 0$): $I_{out} = 4.00$ mA ✓
- pH 14 ($V_{in} = 8.282$ V): $I_{out} = 4 + 16.00 = 20.00$ mA ✓
- pH 7 ($V_{in} = 4.141$ V): $I_{out} = 4 + 8.0 = 12.0$ mA ✓ (mid-scale)

### Step 5 — Temperature Compensation
Because the Nernst slope $S(T) = 2.303 RT/F$ varies with temperature (e.g., 54.20 mV/pH at 0 °C, 74.04 mV/pH at 100 °C), a **Pt1000** temperature sensor is typically co-located with the pH probe, and the microcontroller uses a lookup table or polynomial fit to dynamically correct the slope.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**List any three advantages of the 4–20 mA current loop over 0–10 V voltage signaling in industrial transmitters.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Live-zero fault detection** — 4 mA as lower limit allows immediate detection of a broken wire (which would read 0 mA); a 0 V signal cannot be distinguished from a wire break.
2. **Immunity to cable resistance and noise** — current is set by the transmitter and is independent of wire length, contact resistance, and induced EMI, unlike voltage signals.
3. **Two-wire loop-powered operation** — the same two wires carry both the 4–20 mA signal *and* supply power to the transmitter, simplifying installation and enabling intrinsic safety in hazardous areas.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is a 4-wire (Kelvin) RTD connection preferred for high-accuracy temperature measurement?**

**Model Answer:**
A 4-wire RTD connection uses *two separate pairs* of leads: one pair carries a known excitation current through the RTD, and the other pair senses the voltage drop across the RTD using a high-input-impedance voltmeter. Because essentially no current flows through the voltage-sensing leads, the voltage drop across *their* lead resistance is negligible, and the measured voltage is a true representation of the RTD's resistance. This eliminates both the offset and gain errors introduced by lead resistance, making 4-wire the most accurate connection method, suitable for laboratory and calibration-grade measurements.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — RTD with Lead Wire Compensation + 4–20 mA Design

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 marks]** A Pt100 RTD is connected to a 3-wire Wheatstone bridge with all fixed arms $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ and excitation $V_{ex} = 5$ V. Each lead has resistance $r = 2\ \Omega$. The RTD is at $T = 100\,^\circ\text{C}$. Compute:
(i) The exact RTD resistance using the Callendar-Van Dusen equation.
(ii) The bridge output voltage.
(iii) The percentage gain error introduced by the lead resistance.

**(b) [7 marks]** The bridge output is amplified by an INA of gain $G = 50$ and fed to a V/I converter that produces 4 mA at 0 V and 20 mA at 10 V. The temperature range is 0–200 °C. Compute the loop current when $T = 100\,^\circ\text{C}$.

---

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a)(i) — RTD Resistance at 100 °C
Using the quadratic Callendar-Van Dusen for $T \ge 0\,^\circ\text{C}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_T &= R_0\left[1 + AT + BT^2\right] \\
&= 100\left[1 + 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7}) \times 100^2\right] \\
&= 100\left[1 + 0.39083 - 0.005775\right] \\
&= 100 \times 1.385055 \\
&= 138.51\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

So $\Delta R = 138.51 - 100 = 38.51\ \Omega$.

#### Part (a)(ii) — Bridge Output Voltage
For the 3-wire bridge (one lead in each adjacent arm), the simplified output for small $\Delta R$ is:

$$V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R_0 + r/2} = \frac{5}{4} \cdot \frac{38.51}{100 + 1} = 1.25 \times \frac{38.51}{101} = 0.4766\ \text{V}$$

#### Part (a)(iii) — Gain Error due to Lead Resistance
The *ideal* (no-lead) bridge output is:
$$V_{o,\,ideal} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R_0} = 1.25 \times \frac{38.51}{100} = 0.4814\ \text{V}$$

The percentage gain error is:
$$\%\text{error} = \frac{V_o - V_{o,\,ideal}}{V_{o,\,ideal}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.4766 - 0.4814}{0.4814} \times 100\% = -0.997\%$$

The lead resistance causes a $\approx 1\%$ gain attenuation, which is non-negligible and must be compensated digitally in the transmitter firmware.

#### Part (b) — Loop Current at 100 °C
**Step 1 — Bridge output at 100 °C:** From part (a)(ii), $V_o = 0.4766$ V.
**Step 2 — After INA gain $G = 50$:** $V_{ina} = 50 \times 0.4766 = 23.83$ V.

But the V/I converter accepts only 0–10 V, so we need to introduce a divider. Let the divider ratio be $k = 10/23.83 = 0.4197$.

$V_{in,\,VI} = 0.4766 \times 0.4197 \times 50 = $ — let us re-engineer.

Actually, the proper design: choose $G$ such that $V_{ina,\,max} = 10$ V at the maximum range. With $V_o = 0.4814$ V at $\Delta R = 38.51$ Ω (which is approximately half-span — at 200 °C, $\Delta R \approx 77.04\ \Omega$, $V_o \approx 0.9628$ V). So at *full* range, $V_o \approx 0.9628$ V, and we need $G \times 0.9628 = 10$ V → $G \approx 10.39$.

With $G = 50$ and a voltage divider $k = 0.2078$:
$V_{in,\,VI} = G \cdot k \cdot V_o = 50 \times 0.2078 \times 0.4766 = 4.951$ V.

**Step 3 — V/I conversion:**
$$I_{out} = 4 + \frac{16}{10} \times V_{in,\,VI} = 4 + 1.6 \times 4.951 = 4 + 7.92 = 11.92\,\text{mA}$$

**Verification:** 100 °C is 50% of the 0–200 °C span → expected 4 + 16×0.5 = 12 mA. The small discrepancy (11.92 vs. 12.00) is due to the linear-vs-quadratic RTD curvature, which a digital linearizer in the transmitter would correct.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Capacitance Level Transmitter + pH Signal Conditioning

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 marks]** A capacitance-based level transmitter is used in a cylindrical tank of diameter 2 m, height 3 m, with a vertical rod probe of diameter 20 mm concentric with the tank axis. The liquid is oil with $\varepsilon_{liq} = 2.2$. Calculate the capacitance change when the level rises from 0.5 m to 2.0 m. Assume the rod is bare (non-conducting liquid mode) and the tank wall is grounded.

**(b) [7 marks]** Design the pH signal-conditioning front end for a 0–14 pH range. The Nernst slope is 59.16 mV/pH at 25 °C. Specify (i) the required gain of the instrumentation amplifier so that the output spans 0–10 V; (ii) the output voltage at pH 7; (iii) the V/I converter scaling for 4–20 mA mapping; (iv) the temperature compensation strategy.

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Capacitance Change Between Two Levels

**Step 1 — Geometry.** Coaxial geometry: inner conductor (rod) radius $a = 10$ mm = 0.010 m; outer conductor (tank wall) radius $b = 1.0$ m.

**Step 2 — Capacitance per unit length in air and oil.**
$\ln(b/a) = \ln(1.0/0.010) = \ln(100) = 4.6052$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C'_{air} &= \frac{2\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.0}{4.6052} = 1.208 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F/m} = 12.08\ \text{pF/m} \\
C'_{oil} &= \frac{2\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.2}{4.6052} = 2.658 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F/m} = 26.58\ \text{pF/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total capacitance at each level.**

At $h_1 = 0.5$ m:
$$
C_1 = 12.08 \times 2.5 + 26.58 \times 0.5 = 30.20 + 13.29 = 43.49\ \text{pF}
$$

At $h_2 = 2.0$ m:
$$
C_2 = 12.08 \times 1.0 + 26.58 \times 2.0 = 12.08 + 53.16 = 65.24\ \text{pF}
$$

**Step 4 — Capacitance change.**
$$\Delta C = C_2 - C_1 = 65.24 - 43.49 = 21.75\ \text{pF}$$

**Step 5 — Sensitivity.**
$$\frac{\Delta C}{\Delta h} = \frac{21.75}{1.5} = 14.50\ \text{pF/m}$$

This is the calibration constant for the transmitter's firmware.

#### Part (b) — pH Front-End Design

**(i) Required INA gain.**
Nernst voltage span: $E_{span} = 14 \times 59.16 = 828.24$ mV = 0.828 V.
Required output span: 10 V.
Required gain: $G = 10 / 0.828 = 12.07$.

Use $G = 12$ (set by $R_{gain}$ such that $1 + 2R_1/R_{gain} = 12$ → $R_{gain} = 2R_1/11$).

**(ii) Output voltage at pH 7.**
$E(\text{pH} = 7) = -59.16 \times 7 = -414.12$ mV.
Magnitude: $|V_{out}| = 12 \times 0.41412 = 4.969$ V.

For a *unipolar* 0–10 V output representing pH 0 to 14, we add an offset of +5 V (such that pH 7 → 5 V is mid-scale, and pH 0 → 0 V is the lower end):
$V_{out}(\text{pH} = 7) = 5.000$ V (after offset insertion).

**(iii) V/I converter scaling.**
The V/I converter maps 0 V → 4 mA and 10 V → 20 mA. At pH 7, $V_{in} = 5$ V:
$$I_{out} = 4 + \frac{16}{10} \times 5 = 4 + 8 = 12\,\text{mA}$$

This is exactly mid-scale, as expected for pH 7 (neutral).

**(iv) Temperature compensation strategy.**
A Pt1000 temperature sensor is co-located with the pH probe. The microcontroller reads the temperature and updates the effective Nernst slope:
$$S(T) = 2.303\,RT/F = 0.1984\,T\,\text{(mV/pH, with T in Kelvin)}$$

A polynomial or lookup-table correction is applied in real time. For example, the gain $G$ is dynamically adjusted by a DAC-controlled $R_{gain}$ in the INA, or (more commonly) the digital output of the ADC is multiplied by a temperature-dependent scale factor in firmware before being sent to the DAC that drives the V/I converter. Without this compensation, a measurement at pH 4 and 80 °C would be off by $\approx 30$%.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks on These Topics**
> 1. **Forgetting the 4 mA live-zero offset** when computing currents (most common single error in 4–20 mA problems).
> 2. **Using the *linear* Pt100 approximation** ($\alpha = 0.00385$) for temperatures $> 100\,^\circ\text{C}$, where the quadratic error exceeds the typical KTU-mandated accuracy of ±0.5 °C. Always use the *full* Callendar-Van Dusen for $T > 100\,^\circ\text{C}$.
> 3. **Confusing the 3-wire and 4-wire topologies** — in 3-wire, *one* lead resistance appears in *each* of two adjacent arms, leading to cancellation; in 4-wire, leads are *physically separated* into current and voltage pairs.
> 4. **For Pt100 leads, confusing *resistance* with *reactance* in capacitive level** — RTD is purely resistive; capacitance probe is reactive, excited by AC.
> 5. **pH sign convention errors** — Nernst potential is *negative* for $\text{pH} > \text{pH}_{iso}$. Always state your sign convention explicitly.
> 6. **Not accounting for the temperature dependence of the Nernst slope** — losing 2–3 marks out of 14 in the pH problem.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **4–20 mA loop**: $I_{out} = 4 + 16(x - x_{min})/(x_{max} - x_{min})$; 4 mA = 0% span (live zero); 20 mA = 100% span.
- **Current is set by transmitter**, so it is **independent** of cable length, gauge, and contact resistance.
- **Loop compliance**: $R_{loop,\,max} = (V_{supply} - V_{tx,\,min}) / 20\,\text{mA}$.
- **Pt100 (IEC 60751)**: $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$, $\alpha = 0.00385\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ (linear) or use Callendar-Van Dusen for high accuracy: $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$, $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$.
- **Lead-wire compensation**: 2-wire (none) → 3-wire (Wheatstone bridge) → 4-wire (Kelvin, no error).
- **Capacitance-based level**: linear in $h$ for coaxial probe; non-conducting liquid uses bare probe and liquid as *dielectric*; conducting liquid uses *insulated* probe with liquid as *outer plate*.
- **AC excitation** (10 kHz–1 MHz) is required to measure capacitance; synchronous demodulation recovers the level.
- **pH electrode**: high source impedance ($10^{8}$–$10^{9}\ \Omega$) requires **FET-input INA** ($Z_{in} > 10^{12}\ \Omega$); Nernst slope is **59.16 mV/pH at 25 °C** and varies linearly with absolute temperature.
- **V-to-I converter (Howland pump)**: $I_{out} = V_{in}/R_{sense}$, independent of $R_{load}$.
- **Isopotential pH = 7**: at this pH, electrode potential is independent of temperature (used in smart transmitters for thermal compensation).
- **Industry standard**: ANSI/ISA-50.1 for 4–20 mA; IEC 60751 for Pt100; NAMUR NE 43 for fault current levels (3.6 mA low, 21 mA high).
- **KTU 2024 Scheme specific**: every design must end in 4–20 mA; range, accuracy, and lead-wire compensation are favorite exam topics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
