topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT602 - INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Piping and Instrumentation Diagram. Introduction to Process Piping - Material Selection - Pipe Fitting. Cabling - General Requirements - Cable Types - Cable Segregation - Earthing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)

A **Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** is a detailed schematic representation of the functional relationships between piping, process equipment, instrumentation, and control systems in a process plant. It is the single most important document used in the design, construction, operation, and maintenance of industrial process plants such as refineries, chemical plants, power stations, and pharmaceutical manufacturing facilities.

A P&ID is **NOT** a Process Flow Diagram (PFD). While a PFD shows the broad material and energy flows through major equipment only, a P&ID shows *every pipe, every valve, every instrument, every fitting, and every interlock* needed to construct and operate the plant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> P&IDs are governed by **ISA-5.1** (Instrumentation Society of America — Identification and Symbols for Process Measurement and Control Functions, commonly called "ISA-5.1" or the "Yellow Book"). Every KTU board exam question on P&ID symbols or ISA symbology comes directly from this standard.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a P&ID as the **electrical wiring diagram of a building**. Just as a wiring diagram shows every wire, switch, and outlet connecting lights, fans, and sockets, a P&ID shows every pipe, valve, and instrument connecting tanks, pumps, heat exchangers, and reactors. A building contractor cannot wire a house without a wiring diagram, and a plant engineer cannot build a chemical plant without a P&ID.

## 1.2 Introduction to Process Piping

**Process piping** is the network of pipes, tubes, fittings, valves, and accessories used to convey process fluids (liquids, gases, slurries, steam, or vapors) between process units inside a plant. Process piping is distinct from utility piping (water, air, drains) and from pipework carrying hazardous refrigerants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference for Process Piping Design:**
> - **ASME B31.3** — *Process Piping Code* (most widely used for chemical and petroleum plants)
> - **ASME B31.1** — Power Piping
> - **ASME B31.4** — Pipeline Transportation Systems for Liquid Hydrocarbons
> - **IS 1239 / IS 3589** — Indian Standards for mild steel and steel pipes
> - **ASTM A106, A312, A335** — Material grade specifications

A process pipe is characterized by four primary design parameters:
1. **Nominal Pipe Size (NPS)** — a dimensionless designator (e.g., NPS 2, NPS 6).
2. **Schedule (Sch)** — wall thickness (e.g., Sch 40, Sch 80, Sch 160).
3. **Material Grade** — e.g., A106 Grade B carbon steel.
4. **Pressure Rating / Class** — e.g., 150#, 300#, 600# (ANSI flange class).

## 1.3 Material Selection for Process Piping

Material selection determines whether a piping system will safely contain the process fluid for its design life (typically 15–30 years). Selection is governed by:

| Selection Driver | Typical Consideration |
|---|---|
| **Corrosion resistance** | pH of fluid, presence of chlorides, H₂S, CO₂, oxygen |
| **Temperature** | Cryogenic (-196 °C) to high-temp creep (~650 °C) |
| **Pressure** | Internal design pressure and vacuum |
| **Mechanical strength** | Tensile, yield, fatigue |
| **Weldability** | Carbon equivalent for field welding |
| **Cost** | Capital cost vs. lifecycle cost |
| **Fluid toxicity** | May require higher safety factor |

### Common Piping Materials
- **Carbon Steel (CS, ASTM A106 Gr.B)** — most common; for water, steam, oil, non-corrosive hydrocarbons.
- **Stainless Steel (SS 304 / SS 316 / SS 316L)** — for corrosive chemicals, pharmaceuticals, food.
- **Alloy 20, Monel, Inconel, Hastelloy** — for sulfuric acid, HCl, severe corrosion.
- **PVC / CPVC / PP / PVDF (thermoplastics)** — for chemical drainage, low-pressure acid lines.
- **Ductile Iron** — buried water mains.
- **Glass-lined steel** — for highly corrosive acids at moderate temperatures.

## 1.4 Pipe Fittings

Pipe fittings are components used to **connect, redirect, branch, terminate, or change the size** of pipes. They are classified as:

**By function:**
- **Direction change:** Elbows (90°, 45°, 180° return), Tees, Crosses, Wyes
- **Size change:** Reducers (concentric & eccentric), Bushings
- **Connection / Termination:** Couplings, Unions, Caps, Plugs, Flanges
- **Branch connection:** Tees (equal & reducing), Lateral, Wye
- **Special:** Bends (long radius / short radius), Swages, Nipples

**By manufacturing method:**
- **Butt-weld fittings** (used for high pressure / high temperature)
- **Socket-weld fittings** (used for small-bore, ≤ NPS 2)
- **Threaded fittings** (NPT/BSP — low pressure, utility services)
- **Flanged fittings**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Long Radius (LR) vs. Short Radius (SR) Elbow:**
> - **LR Elbow:** Center-to-end dimension = **1.5 × Nominal Diameter** (preferred for process flow — lower pressure drop).
> - **SR Elbow:** Center-to-end dimension = **1.0 × Nominal Diameter** (used only in space-constrained locations; higher ΔP).

## 1.5 Cabling — General Requirements

Cabling refers to the **electrical conductor infrastructure** that transmits **power, control signals, and data** between field instruments, junction boxes, control panels, marshalling cabinets, and the DCS/PLC. A typical industrial plant may have **hundreds of kilometers of cables**.

General requirements (per IEC 60364, IS 732, IEEE 518):
- Adequate **current carrying capacity** (ampacity) for the connected load.
- Acceptable **voltage drop** (typically ≤ 3% for power, ≤ 1% for control loops).
- **Short-circuit withstand** capability.
- **Mechanical protection** (armour, conduit, tray).
- **Fire survival / flame retardance** (FRLS, LSZH).
- **Chemical resistance** of insulation and sheathing to the plant environment.
- **EMI/EMC compatibility** (shielding, segregation) for instrumentation cables.
- **Earthing / grounding** continuity of armour and shield.

## 1.6 Cable Types

| Cable Type | Construction | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **PVC insulated (1.1 kV grade)** | Cu/Al conductor + PVC insulation + PVC inner sheath + GI armour + PVC outer sheath | LT power distribution |
| **XLPE insulated (1.1 kV / 3.3 kV / 11 kV)** | Cu/Al + XLPE insulation + armour + sheath | HT/LT power, motors |
| **FRLS / LSZH** | Cu + XLPE + FRLS/LSZH sheath | Public buildings, offshore, metros |
| **Instrumentation cable (overall shielded, ISOS)** | Multi-core Cu + PVC/XLPE + Al-Mylar tape + drain wire + armour | 4–20 mA analog signals |
| **Individual pair shielded (ISAS)** | Each pair individually shielded | High-EMI environments, thermocouples |
| **Thermocouple extension cable** | Compensating alloys (KX, JX, etc.) matched to TC type | TC signal transmission |
| **RTD cable** | 3-core or 4-core Cu, low resistance | PT100 RTD signals |
| **Coaxial cable (RG series)** | Center conductor + dielectric + braid | Specialized analog/HF |
| **Cat 6 / Cat 7 (data)** | Twisted pairs + foil + braid | Ethernet, fieldbus (Profibus, Modbus TCP) |
| **Fire-survival cable (FS / MICC)** | Mica tape + XLPE | Critical safety circuits (fire pump, ESD) |
| **Optical fibre cable (OFC)** | Glass fibre + aramid yarn + sheath | Long-distance, EMI-free comms |

## 1.7 Cable Segregation

**Cable segregation** is the physical separation of different categories of cables to prevent:
- **Capacitive / inductive coupling** (noise injection)
- **Electromagnetic interference (EMI)**
- **Cross-talk** between signal and power circuits
- **Heat transfer** from power cables to thermosensitive instrument cables.

Standard segregation (per IEEE 518, IEC 60364, BS 7671):

| Category | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Category 1** | Power > 1 kV | HT cables, motor feeders |
| **Category 2** | Power 50 V – 1 kV | LT feeders, lighting, socket |
| **Category 3** | Control (> 50 V) | Solenoid, relay coils, interlock |
| **Category 4** | Low-level analog / digital signal | 4–20 mA, RTD, TC, fieldbus |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical segregation distances (minimum parallel run):**
> - Power vs. Instrumentation: **≥ 300 mm** (or use grounded metal partition).
> - Signal vs. signal: **≥ 75 mm** unless individually shielded.
> - Crossing angles: **90° preferred** for unavoidable crossovers.
> - Different signal types in **separate trays**.

## 1.8 Earthing (Grounding)

**Earthing** is the connection of conductive parts of an electrical installation to earth (ground) to provide:
1. **Safety** — protect personnel from electric shock on insulation failure.
2. **Equipment protection** — provide a low-impedance fault path to trip protective devices.
3. **Signal integrity** — provide a stable 0 V reference for electronic instrumentation and shield terminations.
4. **Lightning and surge protection** — dissipate transient overvoltages.
5. **EMI control** — drain high-frequency noise from cable shields.

### Types of Earthing Systems (per IEC 60364)
- **TN System (TN-C, TN-S, TN-C-S):** Direct connection of neutral to earth at source.
- **TT System:** Direct connection at source + consumer's own earth electrode.
- **IT System:** Source isolated from earth (used in hospitals, special industrial plants).

### Earthing in Instrumentation
- **Instrument earth (clean earth / IS — Instrumentation Signal):** for signal return and shield termination. Kept isolated from power earth except at one point.
- **Power earth (dirty earth / PE):** for safety of equipment enclosures.
- **Single-point bonding** preferred for instrumentation shield termination to avoid ground loops.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Pitfall:**
> Many students confuse **"earthing" with "grounding"**. In Indian/IEEE practice: **Earthing** = connection to earth (soil) for safety; **Grounding** = establishing 0 V reference for circuits. Both are interconnected but conceptually distinct.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 P&ID Symbolization — ISA-5.1 Core Convention

In ISA-5.1, every instrument is represented by a **circular bubble (or balloon)** connected to the process line. The bubble carries a unique alphanumeric **tag number** (e.g., `FT-101`, `LIC-205`, `PDI-302`).

### Instrument Tag Number Format

$$\text{TAG} = \underbrace{XX}_{\text{Function Letters}} \, - \, \underbrace{YYY}_{\text{Loop Number}}$$

**Function letters (first letter = measured variable, subsequent = modifier / function):**

| Letter | Measured Variable / Function |
|---|---|
| **F** | Flow |
| **L** | Level |
| **P** | Pressure |
| **T** | Temperature |
| **A** | Analysis |
| **V** | Viscosity / Vibration |
| **E** | Voltage / EMF |
| **W** | Weight |
| **I** | Current / Indicate |
| **C** | Control / Conductivity |
| **R** | Record |
| **T** | Transmit |
| **V** | Valve |
| **S** | Switch / Safety |
| **H** | High |
| **L** | Low |
| **E** | Element (primary sensor) |

### Common P&ID Symbols (ISA-5.1)

- **Discrete instrument** — circle with solid line
- **Shared display / DCS** — circle with horizontal line through middle
- **Computer / PLC function** — circle with diagonal line
- **Primary element (orifice, RTD, TC)** — diamond or circle with cross

## 2.2 Process Piping — Theoretical Design Logic

Pipe sizing is governed by a trade-off between **pressure drop (ΔP), velocity, and capital cost**. A correct design satisfies:

1. **Velocity limits** (to prevent erosion, vibration, water hammer)
2. **Pressure drop limit** (to keep pump/compressor head within design)
3. **Two-phase flow regime constraints** (slug, annular, mist)
4. **Erosional velocity** (for solids/slurries)

### Velocity Guidelines (typical)

| Service | Velocity (m/s) |
|---|---|
| Liquid — pump suction | **0.5 – 1.5** |
| Liquid — pump discharge | **1.5 – 3.0** |
| Steam (saturated) | **25 – 40** |
| Compressed air | **10 – 20** |
| Natural gas (high pressure) | **10 – 20** |
| Flare / vent headers | **0.5 × Mach 1** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-grade high-yield** for PCICT602 Module 2.

### Table A — Fluid Mechanics & Pipe Sizing

| # | Formula | Description / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = v \cdot A = v \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$ | Volumetric flow rate (m³/s) |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu} = \dfrac{v \, D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number (dimensionless) |
| 3 | $h_{f} = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g}$ | Darcy–Weisbach friction head loss (m) |
| 4 | $\Delta P = \rho \, g \, h_{f} = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho \, v^{2}}{2}$ | Frictional pressure drop (Pa) |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2.0 \log\!\left( \dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}} \right)$ | Colebrook–White equation (turbulent) |
| 6 | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | Friction factor (laminar, $Re < 2000$) |
| 7 | $V_{erosion} = \dfrac{C}{\sqrt{\rho_{m}}}$ | Erosional velocity API RP 14E (m/s); C ≈ 100 for continuous, 150 for intermittent |
| 8 | $P_{design} = P_{operating} \cdot SF$ | Design pressure with safety factor (1.05–1.1 typical) |
| 9 | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \, SE - 1.2P} + C$ | Barlow's hoop stress — required pipe wall thickness (mm) |
| 10 | $D_{NPS} \approx \text{Inside Diameter (in.)} + 4 \cdot \text{Schedule No.} \cdot 0.025$ | Approx. ID from NPS and Schedule |

**Where:**
- $D$ = internal diameter (m), $L$ = pipe length (m), $v$ = velocity (m/s)
- $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³), $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s), $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity (m²/s)
- $f$ = Darcy friction factor, $\varepsilon$ = pipe roughness (m)
- $S$ = allowable stress (MPa), $E$ = joint efficiency, $C$ = corrosion allowance (mm)

### Table B — Electrical Cabling & Earthing

| # | Formula | Description / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| 11 | $V_{drop} = I \cdot R = I \cdot \dfrac{\rho \, L}{A}$ | DC voltage drop (V) |
| 12 | $V_{drop\,(\%)} = \dfrac{I \cdot L \cdot K}{A}$ | Voltage drop percentage (K = 0.012 for Cu, 0.018 for Al, 3-phase) |
| 13 | $R_{earth} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \cdot \ln\!\left( \dfrac{4L}{d} \right)$ | Earth electrode resistance — single rod (Ω) |
| 14 | $R_{combined} = \dfrac{R_1 \cdot R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Two electrodes in parallel (Ω) |
| 15 | $Z_{surge} = 60 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{f}}$ | Lightning impulse impedance (Ω) |
| 16 | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{sys}}$ | Symmetrical short-circuit current (3-phase) |

> [!NOTE]
> The Colebrook–White equation is **implicit** in $f$. For KTU exam solutions, use the **Swamee–Jain explicit** approximation:
>
> $$f = \dfrac{0.25}{\left[ \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}} \right) \right]^{2}}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **P&IDs** are mandatory in every EPC (Engineering, Procurement, Construction) project. They are legal documents for HAZOP (Hazard and Operability) studies and PSM (Process Safety Management) audits.
- **Process piping** design with ASME B31.3 ensures 15–30 year leak-free service in refineries handling hydrocarbons at 100+ bar and 400+ °C.
- **Cable segregation** is non-negotiable in nuclear and aerospace plants where a single coupled noise pulse can trigger spurious shutdowns.
- **Earthing** keeps a 230 V household shock below 50 mA (let-go threshold) and ensures protective devices trip within **0.4 s** per IEC 60364.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volumetric Flow Rate through a Pipe

**Given:** A pipe of internal diameter $D$ with fluid flowing at mean velocity $v$.

**Step 1:** Identify the cross-sectional area perpendicular to the flow direction. For a circular pipe:

$$A = \pi r^{2} = \pi \left( \dfrac{D}{2} \right)^{2} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the definition of volumetric flow rate. By definition, $Q$ equals the volume crossing the cross-section per unit time:

$$Q = \dfrac{V}{t}$$

**Step 3:** In a small time $dt$, a column of fluid of length $v \cdot dt$ crosses the area $A$. Its volume is:

$$dV = A \cdot v \cdot dt$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the flow rate definition:

$$Q = \dfrac{dV}{dt} = A \cdot v$$

**Step 5:** Insert the area expression:

$$\boxed{Q = v \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}} \quad [\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}]$$

**Conversion to mass flow:**

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = \rho \cdot v \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Darcy–Weisbach Friction Loss (Energy Form)

**Given:** A horizontal pipe of length $L$, internal diameter $D$, conveying a fluid of density $\rho$ at mean velocity $v$, with friction factor $f$.

**Step 1:** Consider a fluid element in the pipe. The mechanical energy balance (Bernoulli + losses) between upstream point 1 and downstream point 2 gives:

$$\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

**Step 2:** For a horizontal constant-diameter pipe, $z_1 = z_2$ and $v_1 = v_2$ (continuity). The kinetic and potential terms cancel, so:

$$\dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = h_L \quad \Rightarrow \quad h_f = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho g}$$

**Step 3:** The head loss is empirically proportional to the kinetic energy of the fluid and the relative pipe length:

$$h_f = k \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g}$$

where $k$ is a dimensionless coefficient that depends on flow regime. Setting $k = f$ (Darcy friction factor):

$$h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g} \quad [\text{m of fluid}]$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by $\rho g$ to obtain pressure form:

$$\boxed{\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho \, v^{2}}{2}} \quad [\text{Pa}]$$

**Step 5:** Compute the power lost to friction (pumping penalty):

$$P_{loss} = \Delta P \cdot Q = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho \, v^{2}}{2} \cdot v \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$

Simplify:

$$P_{loss} = \dfrac{\pi f \rho v^{3} L D}{8} \quad [\text{W}]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Pressure drop scales with $v^{2}$ (laminar) and $v^{3}$ in pumping power. Hence the classical **"flow optimization"** in chemical plants — pipe sizes are chosen such that incremental pipe cost = incremental pump operating cost over plant life.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Voltage Drop in a Cable (3-Phase)

**Given:** A 3-phase, 4-wire system with balanced load current $I$ per phase, one-way cable length $L$ (per phase), conductor cross-section $A$, material resistivity $\rho$.

**Step 1:** Resistance of one phase conductor (one-way length):

$$R = \rho \cdot \dfrac{L}{A} \quad [\Omega]$$

**Step 2:** In a 3-phase system, the voltage drop between two phases is determined by the **vector difference** of two phase voltages displaced by 120°. The result is:

$$V_{drop,\,L-L} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot R \cdot \cos\phi$$

For predominantly resistive loads, $\cos\phi \approx 1$, so:

$$V_{drop} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot R$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $R$:

$$V_{drop} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot \rho \cdot \dfrac{L}{A}$$

**Step 4:** Introduce the **specific resistance constant** $K$ for convenience. For copper at 20 °C in a 3-phase system, the engineering constant is $K = 0.012 \, \Omega \cdot \text{mm}^{2}/\text{m}$ (combines $\sqrt{3}$, $\rho_{Cu}$, and unit conversion):

$$\boxed{V_{drop} = \dfrac{I \cdot L \cdot K}{A}} \quad [\text{V}]$$

**Step 5:** Voltage drop percentage relative to nominal line voltage $V_L$:

$$V_{drop\,(\%)} = \dfrac{V_{drop}}{V_L} \times 100 = \dfrac{I \cdot L \cdot K}{A \cdot V_L} \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** The 3-phase constant $K$ is **$\sqrt{3}$ times** the 1-phase DC constant. Many students forget the $\sqrt{3}$ factor and lose 2 marks.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Earth Electrode Resistance (Rod in Uniform Soil)

**Given:** A vertical earth rod of length $L$ and diameter $d$, fully driven into uniform soil of resistivity $\rho$.

**Step 1:** The classic derivation assumes the rod carries current $I$ uniformly into a hemispherical equipotential surface in the soil. The current density at radius $r$ is:

$$J(r) = \dfrac{I}{2\pi r^{2}}$$

**Step 2:** The potential gradient is $E = \rho \cdot J$. The potential difference between the rod surface (radius $r_0 = d/2$) and a remote point is:

$$V = \int_{r_0}^{\infty} \rho \cdot \dfrac{I}{2\pi r^{2}} \, dr = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi r_0} = \dfrac{\rho I}{\pi d}$$

**Step 3:** The resistance is $R = V/I$:

$$R = \dfrac{\rho}{\pi d}$$

**Step 4:** For finite-length rods (not infinitely long), the formula is corrected by an empirical length factor (Dwight's formula):

$$\boxed{R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\!\left( \dfrac{4L}{d} \right) - 1 \right]}$$

**Step 5:** For multiple rods in parallel (separated by ≥ rod length), resistances combine approximately in parallel:

$$R_{eq} = \dfrac{R_{rod}}{N}$$

More accurately, with mutual interference, a **spacing factor** $\lambda$ is introduced:

$$R_{eq} = \dfrac{R_{rod}}{N} \cdot (1 + \lambda) \quad ; \quad \lambda \approx 0.1 \text{ to } 0.3$$

---

| Service | Fluid | T (°C) | P (bar) | Recommended Material |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cooling water | H₂O + Cl⁻ | 5–60 | 3–6 | **Carbon Steel** (A106 Gr.B) |
| Steam — HP | Steam | 300–540 | 40–100 | **Carbon Steel** (A335 P11/P22 alloy for > 540 °C) |
| Sulphuric acid (98%) | H₂SO₄ | 20–60 | 1–3 | **Carbon Steel** (conc. H₂SO₄ is passivating) |
| Sulphuric acid (dilute) | H₂SO₄ + H₂O | 20–80 | 1–3 | **SS 316 / Alloy 20 / PVDF** |
| Hydrochloric acid | HCl | 20–60 | 1–3 | **Hastelloy B/C / PVDF / Rubber-lined CS** |
| Caustic soda (30%) | NaOH | 20–100 | 1–5 | **Carbon Steel** (CS resists caustic up to ~50%) |
| Demineralized water | H₂O (high purity) | 20–60 | 1–3 | **SS 304L / SS 316L** (avoid CS corrosion) |
| Cryogenic LNG | CH₄ | -162 | 5–10 | **SS 304L** (austenitic, low-temp ductile) |
| Sea water | H₂O + NaCl | 5–35 | 2–6 | **Super-duplex SS / Cu-Ni 90/10** |
| Pharmaceutical WFI | H₂O | 20–80 | 1–3 | **SS 316L, electropolished, Ra ≤ 0.4 µm** |
| Slurry (ore) | Solid + liquid | 20–50 | 2–10 | **HDPE / Rubber-lined / Polyurethane-lined CS** |
| Hydrogen sulfide | H₂S (wet) | 20–100 | 5–30 | **NACE MR0175 compliant** SS / alloy (SCC resistant) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] 
**Define a Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID). List any four symbols used in P&IDs as per ISA-5.1 standard.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** is a detailed schematic diagram that depicts the interconnection of process equipment, piping, valves, instrumentation, and control systems used in the design, construction, and operation of a process plant. It is drawn as per **ISA-5.1** standard and serves as the master document for construction, commissioning, operation, and HAZOP studies.

**Four ISA-5.1 Symbols:**
1. **Circle (Bubble)** — represents an instrument (discrete, shared, or computer).
2. **Diamond** — represents a primary element (orifice, RTD, thermocouple).
3. **Square / Rectangle with diagonal** — represents a DCS / PLC.
4. **Letter codes inside circle** — denote instrument function (F, P, T, L, A, etc.) and loop number.

---

### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] 
**What is cable segregation? State the standard minimum spacing between power and instrumentation cable trays.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Cable segregation** is the physical separation of different categories of electrical and instrumentation cables (HT power, LT power, control, low-level signal, data) by distinct trays, conduits, or maintained spacing distances, to prevent:
- Electromagnetic interference (EMI)
- Inductive / capacitive coupling
- Cross-talk and noise injection
- Heat damage to thermosensitive signal cables

**Standard minimum spacing:**
- Between **power cable tray and instrumentation cable tray** running parallel: **≥ 300 mm** (or use a continuous grounded metal partition).
- Between two instrumentation trays of different signals: **≥ 75 mm** unless individually shielded.
- Crossovers should be at **90°**.

---

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) with INTERNAL CHOICE

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] 
**With neat sketches, explain the various types of pipe fittings used in process piping. Differentiate clearly between concentric and eccentric reducers.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Pipe fittings are piping components used to **connect, redirect, branch, terminate, or change the size** of pipes. They are manufactured as **butt-weld, socket-weld, or threaded** types.

**Types of Pipe Fittings:**

1. **Elbows** — change direction.
   - 90° LR (Long Radius, 1.5D)
   - 90° SR (Short Radius, 1.0D)
   - 45° LR/SR
   - 180° return bend

2. **Tees** — branch connection.
   - Equal tee (straight = branch)
   - Reducing tee (straight ≠ branch)

3. **Reducers** — change pipe size.
   - **Concentric** reducer: centerlines aligned; used in **vertical lines** to avoid air pocket.
   - **Eccentric** reducer: one side flat (top or bottom); used in **horizontal lines** to maintain top-of-pipe or bottom-of-pipe flat for drainage or air venting.

4. **Caps and Plugs** — terminate pipe ends.

5. **Couplings and Unions** — connect two pipes; union allows disconnection.

6. **Flanges** — bolted detachable joint (Weld Neck, Slip-On, Blind, Threaded, Socket-Weld).

7. **Specials** — Laterals, Wyes, Swages, Bends (hot/cold formed).

**Concentric vs. Eccentric Reducer Diagram:**

```
CONCENTRIC REDUCER                    ECCENTRIC REDUCER
(Vertical line — preferred)           (Horizontal line — preferred)
                                      
   |  ___                              |  ___
   | /   \                             | /   \
   |/     \                            |/     \___
   |       \                           |          
   |________\                          |__________
     ↓ alignment                        ↓ flat-side up
        (centers match)                  (for air venting)
```

**Eccentric reducer** is used in **horizontal pump suction** so that air is not trapped in the line, and in **drain lines** to maintain self-draining.

**Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):**
- 2 marks — definition + classification
- 2 marks — sketches of all fittings
- 2 marks — concentric vs. eccentric explanation
- 1 mark — real-world application example

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] 
**Explain the criteria for material selection of process piping. Discuss the suitability of carbon steel, stainless steel, and exotic alloys for different process services.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Material Selection Criteria:**

The choice of piping material is determined by the following:

| Criterion | Description |
|---|---|
| **1. Corrosion** | pH, chlorides, H₂S, CO₂, O₂ content |
| **2. Temperature** | Cryogenic to high-temp creep |
| **3. Pressure** | Internal design pressure + safety factor |
| **4. Mechanical** | Tensile/yield strength, fatigue |
| **5. Weldability** | Carbon equivalent (CE) < 0.45 for field weld |
| **6. Cost** | Capital + lifecycle cost |
| **7. Safety** | Toxicity, fire rating |

**Material Suitability:**

**(i) Carbon Steel (CS) — ASTM A106 Gr.B, A53, A333:**
- **Most common** material in process plants.
- Used for **water, steam, oil, non-corrosive hydrocarbons, lube oil, fuel gas**.
- Inexpensive, easily welded, readily available.
- **Limitation:** Not suitable for acidic (low pH) or chloride-bearing fluids (corrosion), or for very low-temperature service (becomes brittle below -29 °C unless normalized).

**(ii) Stainless Steel (SS) — 304, 304L, 316, 316L:**
- **Austenitic SS 304**: general corrosion resistance, food, pharma, low-temp (-196 °C LNG).
- **SS 316/316L**: contains 2–3% Mo for improved resistance to **chlorides, pitting, and crevice corrosion**; used in marine, chemical, and pharmaceutical services.
- **L-grade** (low carbon ≤ 0.03%) prevents carbide precipitation and intergranular corrosion in welded joints.
- Used for **WFI (water for injection), clean utilities, nitric acid, dilute sulfuric acid**.

**(iii) Exotic Alloys:**
- **Alloy 20 (Carpenter 20)**: for sulfuric acid service.
- **Monel 400**: for hydrofluoric acid and sea water.
- **Inconel 600/625**: high-temp (up to 1100 °C), oxidizing atmospheres.
- **Hastelloy B/C**: for HCl, wet HCl, severe reducing services.
- **Titanium**: for chlorinated brine, sea water condensers, nitric acid.
- **Duplex / Super-Duplex (22% Cr)**: for sea water, high-pressure, high-chloride offshore.
- **NACE MR0175 compliant alloys**: mandatory for sour service (H₂S) in oil & gas.

**Mark Distribution:**
- 2 marks — selection criteria list
- 2 marks — CS suitability
- 2 marks — SS suitability
- 1 mark — exotic alloys and special services

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] 
**Explain the general requirements of industrial cabling. With a neat table, classify various types of cables used in process industries.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**General Requirements of Industrial Cabling:**

1. **Current Carrying Capacity (Ampacity):** Cable must carry continuous load current without exceeding the conductor temperature limit (90 °C for XLPE, 70 °C for PVC).
2. **Voltage Drop:** Should be limited to **≤ 3%** for power circuits and **≤ 1%** for control / instrumentation circuits at full load.
3. **Short-Circuit Withstand:** Cable insulation and conductor must withstand $I^{2}t$ of the maximum short-circuit current for the protective device clearing time.
4. **Mechanical Protection:** Steel armour (SWA), conduit, or HDPE ducting protects against impact and crushing.
5. **Fire Performance:** FRLS (Flame Retardant Low Smoke) or LSZH (Low Smoke Zero Halogen) sheathing to limit toxic gas emission in fires.
6. **Chemical Resistance:** Sheath and insulation must resist oils, acids, alkalis, and solvents of the plant environment.
7. **EMI/EMC Compatibility:** Shielded instrumentation cables to reject external electromagnetic noise.
8. **Temperature Rating:** Conductor and insulation rated for ambient + temperature rise due to load.
9. **Earthing Continuity:** Armour or shield must be earthed at both ends (or one end with proper consideration).
10. **Bending Radius:** Minimum bend radius = **12 × OD** for multi-core power, **8 × OD** for single-core.
11. **Ingress Protection:** Cables routed through classified areas (Ex zones) must meet IP/IS/IECEx requirements.
12. **Standards Compliance:** IS 694, IS 1554, IS 7098, IEC 60502, IEEE 1580, BS 5467.

**Cable Classification Table:**

| Cable Type | Voltage Class | Insulation | Sheath | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LT PVC power** | 1.1 kV | PVC | PVC | Lighting, sockets, LT feeders |
| **LT XLPE power** | 1.1 kV | XLPE | PVC / FRLS | Motor feeders, MCC |
| **HT XLPE power** | 3.3 / 6.6 / 11 / 22 / 33 kV | XLPE | PVC / FRLS | HT motors, transformers |
| **FRLS / LSZH** | 1.1 kV | XLPE | LSZH | Public, offshore, metros |
| **Control cable** | 1.1 kV | PVC/XLPE | PVC | Relay logic, interlock |
| **Instrumentation (ISOS)** | 300 V | PVC/XLPE | Armoured PVC, overall Al-Mylar shield | 4–20 mA loops |
| **Individual pair shielded (ISAS)** | 300 V | PVC/XLPE | Armoured PVC, per-pair shield | TC, RTD, noisy plants |
| **Thermocouple extension** | 300 V | PVC / fiberglass | Armour | TC signal |
| **RTD cable** | 300 V | PVC / silicone | Armour | PT100 signals |
| **Fire-survival (MICC / FS)** | 1.1 kV | XLPE + mica | LSZH | Fire pumps, ESD |
| **Data — Cat 6 / 7** | < 50 V | PE | LSZH | Ethernet, fieldbus |
| **Fibre optic (OFC)** | N/A | Glass fibre | PE / LSZH | Long distance, EMI-free |

**Mark Distribution:**
- 3 marks — general requirements (key 6–7 points)
- 3 marks — cable classification table
- 1 mark — standard reference

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] 
**Explain the importance of cable segregation. State the various categories of cables and the minimum segregation distances. Briefly describe different types of earthing systems with their importance in instrumentation.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Importance of Cable Segregation:**

Cable segregation prevents **electromagnetic interference, capacitive coupling, inductive coupling, ground loops, and heat damage** between power and signal cables. Without segregation:
- A 4–20 mA signal cable laid parallel to a 415 V motor feeder picks up 50 Hz mains hum → controller malfunction.
- A high-voltage cable induces voltage spikes in nearby TC extension cable → false temperature readings.
- Heat from a power cable can degrade the insulation of a thermosensitive signal cable.

**Categories of Cables and Segregation Distances (per IEEE 518 / IEC 60364):**

| Category | Cable Class | Examples | Min. Spacing to Instrumentation Tray |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cat 1** | HT Power (> 1 kV) | 11 kV, 6.6 kV motor | **≥ 600 mm** + metal partition |
| **Cat 2** | LT Power (50 V – 1 kV) | 415 V MCC, lighting | **≥ 300 mm** |
| **Cat 3** | Control (> 50 V AC) | Solenoid, relay, interlock | **≥ 150 mm** |
| **Cat 4** | Low-level analog / digital | 4–20 mA, RTD, TC, fieldbus | Trayed separately |

**Best Practices:**
- Use **separate trays** for each category.
- Use **continuous metallic barriers** (steel dividers) where spacing is constrained.
- Cross power and signal cables at **90°**.
- Earth both ends of power armour; **single-point earth** for instrumentation shield (to avoid ground loops).

**Earthing Systems:**

Earthing (grounding) provides a **low-impedance path** for fault currents and a **stable reference potential** for electronic circuits.

**Types of Earthing Systems (IEC 60364):**

1. **TN System** (Terra-Neutral)
   - **TN-C:** Combined PEN conductor (single neutral + earth).
   - **TN-S:** Separate PE and N conductors throughout.
   - **TN-C-S:** Combined at source, separate in installation (PME — Protective Multiple Earthing).

2. **TT System**
   - Direct earthing at source **AND** at consumer (independent earth electrodes).
   - Used in rural, agricultural, temporary installations.

3. **IT System**
   - Source **isolated from earth** (or high-impedance earthed).
   - First fault does not trip; used in **hospitals, mines, special industrial plants**.

**Importance in Instrumentation:**

- **Safety:** Personnel protection from shock on insulation failure; limits touch voltage to < 50 V.
- **Equipment Protection:** Fault current path trips MCB/ELCB within 0.4 s.
- **Signal Integrity:** Stable 0 V reference for analog signals (4–20 mA loops). Single-point earthing of cable shields prevents ground loops.
- **Noise Rejection:** Shields of instrumentation cables drain high-frequency noise to earth.
- **Lightning / Surge:** Low-impedance earth dissipates transient overvoltages from lightning strikes or switching surges.
- **EMC Compliance:** Mandatory for CE-marked equipment per IEC 61000.

**Mark Distribution:**
- 2 marks — importance of segregation
- 2 marks — categories + spacing table
- 2 marks — types of earthing systems
- 1 mark — instrumentation earthing importance

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ factor** in 3-phase voltage drop calculations (costs 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing concentric and eccentric reducer applications** — most students say "concentric is for horizontal" which is **WRONG**. Concentric is for vertical; eccentric is for horizontal (pump suction to avoid air pocket).
> 3. **Using IV (current × voltage) instead of $I^{2}R$** when computing power loss in cables.
> 4. **Writing "earthing = grounding"** without distinguishing signal earth from protective earth.
> 5. **Omitting the schedule number** when stating pipe dimensions — always write **NPS × Sch** (e.g., NPS 6, Sch 40).
> 6. **Forgetting to state ISA-5.1** when explaining P&ID symbols — examiners expect the standard reference.
> 7. **Confusing long-radius (1.5D) and short-radius (1.0D) elbows** — LR is always preferred for process flow.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **P&ID** is drawn as per **ISA-5.1**; uses **bubble symbols** with **letter codes** (F, P, L, T, A, etc.) and **loop numbers**.
- ✅ **Process piping design** follows **ASME B31.3**; sized using **velocity limits** and **Darcy–Weisbach** pressure drop.
- ✅ **NPS + Schedule** uniquely define a pipe; remember **NPS ≈ ID only for Sch 40 up to NPS 12**.
- ✅ **Material selection** is governed by **corrosion, temperature, pressure, weldability, cost**.
- ✅ **Carbon Steel** for water/steam/oil; **SS 316/316L** for chemicals/pharma; **exotic alloys** for HCl, H₂SO₄, sea water, H₂S.
- ✅ **Long-Radius elbow = 1.5D** (process); **Short-Radius elbow = 1.0D** (space-constrained only).
- ✅ **Concentric reducer** → vertical lines; **Eccentric reducer** → horizontal lines (pump suction).
- ✅ **Butt-weld fittings** for high pressure; **Socket-weld** for small bore ≤ NPS 2; **Threaded** for utilities.
- ✅ **Cabling standards:** IS 694, IS 1554, IS 7098, IEC 60502, IEEE 1580.
- ✅ **Cable categories:** HT Power → LT Power → Control → Instrumentation → Data.
- ✅ **Segregation distance:** Power to Instrumentation tray **≥ 300 mm** (parallel), cross at **90°**.
- ✅ **Voltage drop limit:** ≤ 3% power, ≤ 1% control/instrumentation.
- ✅ **Earthing types (IEC 60364):** TN-C, TN-S, TN-C-S, TT, IT.
- ✅ **Instrumentation earthing** uses **single-point bonding** of shields; **separated IS and PE** except at one reference point.
- ✅ **Earthing resistance** target: **< 1 Ω** for large substations, **< 5 Ω** for industrial plants, **< 10 Ω** for small installations.
- ✅ **Barlow's formula:** $t = \dfrac{PD}{2SE - 1.2P} + C$ for pipe wall thickness.
- ✅ **Darcy–Weisbach:** $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$.
- ✅ **3-phase voltage drop:** $V_{drop} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot R$ (don't forget the $\sqrt{3}$).
- ✅ **Earth rod resistance (Dwight's):** $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
