## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**Define instrument power distribution. List the standard voltage levels used in industrial instrumentation systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Instrument power distribution refers to the engineered electrical system that supplies conditioned, regulated, and uninterrupted power to all field instruments, control panels, and I/O subsystems in a plant. The hierarchy includes grid supply, UPS, battery bank, and DG set.

Standard voltage levels (1.5 marks):
- **24 V DC** — Two-wire transmitter loop supply (most common, ISA standard)
- **48 V DC** — Telecom and remote I/O systems
- **110 V AC** — Older US/UK control room equipment supply
- **230 V AC single-phase / 415 V AC three-phase** — European/Indian mains standard (1.5 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**List any four types of control panels and state one specific application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Console Panel** — Used in main control rooms for operator interface with DCS/HMI stations. (0.75 mark)
2. **Cabinet/Enclosed Panel** — Used in process field areas for MCC and local control; provides IP-55 protection. (0.75 mark)
3. **Wall-Mounted Panel** — Used where floor space is limited; for sub-panels and small process skids. (0.75 mark)
4. **Marshalling/Relay Panel** — Used for I/O interconnections, terminal blocks, and signal routing between field and DCS. (0.75 mark)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Explain the complete architecture of an instrument power distribution system for a critical process plant. With a neat block diagram, describe the function of each block: Grid supply, APFC, Transformer, UPS (online double conversion), Battery bank, DG set, Distribution boards, 24V DC SMPS, and Field instrument supply.**

**Model Answer (14 marks — full marks breakdown):**

**(a) Introduction & Block Diagram (4 marks):**

The instrument power distribution system for a critical process plant must guarantee **99.99% availability** with no single point of failure. It follows a **dual-redundant architecture** with physical separation of BPCS (Basic Process Control System) and SIS (Safety Instrumented System) supplies as per IEC 61508/61511.

```
Grid → Main LT Panel → APFC → Transformer → Bus A (UPS-A) ─┐
                              ↘ Bus B (UPS-B) ──────────────┤
DG Set (auto-start) ──────────────────────────── Both Buses ←┘
Batteries (30 min) ──────────── each UPS
24V DC SMPS (redundant) → Marshalling → Field Loops
```

**(b) Functional Description of Each Block (8 marks — 0.5 mark each + key function):**

1. **Grid Supply (11 kV/415 V):** Primary utility power; feeds Main LT Panel through HT switchgear and transformer.

2. **Main LT Panel:** Receives 415 V AC, distributes via ACBs (Air Circuit Breakers) and bus-coupler; contains power monitoring (kWh, PF, harmonics).

3. **APFC Panel (Automatic Power Factor Correction):** Maintains power factor > 0.95 by switching capacitor banks; reduces penalty from utility and improves voltage regulation.

4. **Distribution Transformer (415/230 V):** Steps down for single-phase loads; typically dry-type cast resin for indoor installation (K-rated for harmonics).

5. **Bus A and Bus B (Dual Bus):** Physically separated redundant paths; each rated for full plant load.

6. **UPS-A and UPS-B (Online Double Conversion):** Continuously converts AC→DC→AC; provides **±1% voltage regulation**, frequency stability (50 Hz ± 0.5%), and **zero transfer time** on grid failure. Bypass static switch for maintenance.

7. **Battery Bank (SMF VRLA, 30 min):** Floats on DC bus; supplies inverter during grid failure; sized as:
$$
\text{Ah} = \frac{P_{load} \times t_{backup}}{V_{bat} \times \eta_{inv} \times 0.8}
$$

8. **Diesel Generator (DG) Set (N+1 redundant):** Auto-start within 10–15 seconds of grid failure; provides 8-hour continuous autonomy; changeover via ATS (Automatic Transfer Switch).

9. **Distribution Boards (DB):** MCB/MCCB protected sub-circuits per panel/area; selective coordination prevents cascade tripping.

10. **24 V DC SMPS (Switch Mode Power Supply):** Convert 230 V AC → 24 V DC; redundant N+1 configuration; DIN-rail mounted; typically 10 A rating with 150% peak load capacity.

11. **Marshalling Cabinet:** Houses terminal blocks, fuse terminals, signal conditioners; interfaces between field and control panel.

12. **Field Instrument Supply (24 V DC):** Two-wire loop powering 4–20 mA transmitters; intrinsically safe barriers in hazardous areas.

**(c) Standards and Redundancy Philosophy (2 marks):**
- **IEC 61508/61511** — SIS power must be independent of BPCS
- **IEEE 1100** — Grounding and bonding practices
- **ISA RP 42.1** — Loop power sizing
- **Redundancy:** Hot standby, N+1, 2-out-of-2 voting for critical loads

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice**

**Explain in detail the various types of control panels used in industrial instrumentation. Compare console, cabinet, wall-mounted, and open-frame panels on the basis of application, IP rating, operator interface, and maintenance access. With suitable sketches, describe a typical console panel layout.**

**Model Answer (14 marks):**

**(a) Introduction (2 marks):**

Control panels are the physical enclosures that house instruments, controllers, recorders, indicators, relays, and terminal blocks. They are designed considering operator ergonomics, equipment protection (IP rating), maintenance accessibility, and signal integrity. Panel selection depends on location (control room vs field), operator interface requirements, environmental conditions, and cost.

**(b) Types of Control Panels (6 marks — 1.5 marks each):**

1. **Console Panel:** Desk-angled structure, 1500–1800 mm tall, with two wing panels meeting at 110° at the operator's apex. The angled face houses indicators, recorders, and push buttons within the operator's natural sightline. Used in main control rooms for DCS operations. IP-54 typical. Excellent operator interface but requires large floor footprint.

2. **Cabinet / Enclosed Panel:** Free-standing, floor-mounted, 2000–2200 mm tall, with hinged front and rear doors. Made of CRCA steel (2 mm) powder-coated (RAL 7035). Houses bulk instrumentation, relays, and power distribution. Provides IP-55 to IP-65 protection. Maintenance access only from front; rear door for cabling.

3. **Wall-Mounted Panel:** Bolted to wall, 800–1200 mm tall, slim depth (300–400 mm). Used where floor space is limited (e.g., MCC rooms, local control stations). IP-65 typical. Operator interface limited; mainly for housing starters and small PLCs.

4. **Open-Frame / Rack Panel:** 19-inch standard (EIA-310-D) with vertical mounting rails; no doors, no side panels. Used in clean indoor environments (server rooms, telecom). IP-20 only. Full 360° access; best for hot-running equipment needing natural convection cooling.

**(c) Comparison Matrix (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Console | Cabinet | Wall-Mounted | Open-Frame |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Application | Main control room | Process field, MCC | Local control, skid | Server, telecom |
| IP Rating | IP-54 | IP-55/65 | IP-65 | IP-20 |
| Operator Interface | Excellent | Good | Poor | N/A |
| Maintenance Access | Front + sides | Front only | Front only | All sides |
| Cost (per m²) | Highest | Medium | Low | Lowest |
| Floor Footprint | Large | Medium | Minimal | Low |

**(d) Console Panel Layout (2 marks):**

```
        ┌────────────────────────────────┐
        │     Left Wing (110° angle)     │
        │  [Indicators] [Recorders]      │
        │  [Push Buttons] [Annunciators]  │
        └────────────────────────────────┘
                       │
                       │ (Apex — Operator Position)
                       │
        ┌────────────────────────────────┐
        │     Right Wing (110° angle)    │
        │  [Controllers] [PC/Mimic]      │
        │  [Selector Switches] [Lamps]    │
        └────────────────────────────────┘
        ┌──────────────────────┐
        │  Keyboard Drawer     │
        │  CPU/Terminal Compartment (below) │
        └──────────────────────┘
```

The operator sits at the apex with **optimal viewing angle < 30°** for all critical instruments, following **ergonomic standards (ISO 9241, EN 614-1)**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in This Topic (KTU Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Confusing 4-20 mA with 0-20 mA** — The lower limit is 4 mA (not 0) because 4 mA represents "live zero," allowing fault detection (current < 4 mA = open loop). Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Wrong HART frequency** — HART uses **1200 Hz (mark) and 2200 Hz (space)** FSK, **NOT** 1000/2000 Hz. Students often confuse this.
> 3. **IP rating digit order** — IP-65 means **6 = dust-tight, 5 = water jets**. Reversing the digits is a 1-mark cut.
> 4. **Forgetting DG autonomy** — KTU expects **8 hours** minimum DG backup for critical plants. Saying "few hours" loses 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Mixing 24V DC and 110V AC** — The standard 4-20 mA loop is **24 V DC** (not 110 V AC). Confusion costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Skipping HART vs Fieldbus distinction** — HART is **hybrid** (analog + digital on same wire); Fieldbus is **purely digital**. Examiners want this distinction explicitly.
> 7. **No numerical example in derivations** — Any 14-mark question on loops or lighting **must have a worked-out numerical example**. Pure theory gets max 8/14.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Instrument Power, Lighting, Communication & Panels:**

### 🔌 Instrument Power
- Standard loop voltage: **24 V DC** (4–20 mA)
- Compliance voltage formula: $V = V_s - IR$
- Power pyramid: Grid → Transformer → UPS → Battery → DG → 24V SMPS → Loop
- UPS types: Offline (VFD), Line-interactive, **Online double-conversion** (best)
- Battery backup: **30 min minimum**; DG: **8 hours** autonomy
- SIS power must be **physically independent** of BPCS (IEC 61511)

### 💡 Control Room Lighting
- Illuminance: **300–500 lux** general, **500–750 lux** on panels
- Color temperature: **4000 K** cool white; CRI ≥ **80**
- UGR < **19** for VDU areas
- Inverse square law: $E = I/d^2$
- Luminaires formula: $N = EA/(\Phi \cdot UF \cdot MF)$
- Emergency lighting: **30–50 lux**, 90 min battery backup

### 📡 Communication System
- **HART** = 4–20 mA + 1200/2200 Hz FSK at 1200 bps
- **Foundation Fieldbus H1** = 31.25 kbps, two-wire, 32 devices
- **Profibus DP** = 12 Mbps, RS-485, 126 devices
- **Modbus RTU/TCP** = open protocol, RS-485/Ethernet
- **ISA-95 Pyramid:** Level 0 (Field) → 1 (Control) → 2 (Supervisory) → 3 (Operations) → 4 (Enterprise)

### 🗄️ Control Panel Types
- **Console** = main control room, angled wings, apex seating
- **Cabinet** = free-standing, IP-55/65, field/MCC
- **Wall-mounted** = space-saving, IP-65
- **Open-frame** = 19-inch, IP-20, server/telecom
- **Marshalling** = I/O interconnections, terminal blocks only
- IP code: First digit = solids, second = liquids (e.g., IP-65)

### 🗄️ Auxiliary Racks & Cabinets
- **19-inch rack** standard (EIA-310-D): 1U = 44.45 mm
- Standard rack: **42U** = 1870 mm tall
- Heat dissipation: every 10 °C rise halves MTBF
- Marshalling cabinets: terminals, fuses, signal conditioners, relays

### 🔧 Panel Piping & Tubing
- **Tube materials:** Copper (ASTM B-280), SS 304/316, Monel
- **Standard sizes:** 1/4" OD, 3/8" OD, 1/2" OD (0.035" wall)
- **Barlow's formula:** $P = 2St/D$ for pressure rating
- **FRL unit:** Filter-Regulator-Lubricator for instrument air
- **Pneumatic signal:** 3–15 psi (or 0.2–1.0 kg/cm²)
- Leak test: **soap solution + 1.5× working pressure for 10 min**

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Mnemonic for Panel Selection:**
> **"C-C-W-O-M"** → **C**onsole, **C**abinet, **W**all-mounted, **O**pen-frame, **M**arshalling — in decreasing order of operator interface quality.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Air Headers

### Formal Definition
An **Air Header** is a metallic manifold (typically constructed from carbon steel, stainless steel, or copper) that acts as the central distribution point for **Instrument Air** within a process plant or control room. It receives pressurized, cleaned, and dried air from the air compressor and receiver tank, and distributes it to multiple downstream branches supplying individual pneumatic instruments, valves, and actuators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The air header is the *single point of failure boundary* for the entire pneumatic instrumentation network. Its design determines the reliability of every downstream pneumatic device.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of an air header like a **water tap manifold in a hotel** — instead of running individual pipes from the main municipal supply to each bathroom, one large pipe (the header) takes the high-pressure supply, and smaller pipes branch off to serve each fixture. The header ensures equal pressure, provides isolation valves, and allows maintenance on one branch without shutting down the whole system.

### Key Specifications
- **Working Pressure:** 5.5 to 7.0 bar (typical industrial standard: **6 bar / 90 psig**)
- **Header Diameter:** 2" to 6" depending on flow load
- **Branch Connections:** 1/2" NPT or 1/4" NPT with isolation ball valves
- **Pressure Gauges & Safety Relief Valves:** Mandatory on every header

### Critical Air Quality Parameters (ISO 8573-1)
| Parameter | Class 1 (Best) | Class 2 (Standard) | Class 3 (Acceptable) |
|-----------|----------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| Particle Size | **0.1 μm** | 1 μm | 5 μm |
| Dew Point | **-70°C** | -40°C | -20°C |
| Oil Content | **0.01 mg/m³** | 0.1 mg/m³ | 1 mg/m³ |

---

## 1.2 Graphic Displays (HMI / MMI)

### Formal Definition
A **Graphic Display** in industrial automation refers to the **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** or **Man-Machine Interface (MMI)** — a software-based visualization system that graphically represents process variables, equipment status, alarms, and control loops in real time, allowing operators to monitor and interact with the plant.

### Types of Graphic Displays

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Classification:**
> 1. **Mimic Displays:** Process flow diagrams with live values, often a wall-mounted panel board.
> 2. **Overview Displays:** Plant-wide summary screens (Level 1).
> 3. **Group / Area Displays:** Subsystem or unit-level screens (Level 2).
> 4. **Loop / Detail Displays:** Single PID loop with tuning parameters (Level 3).
> 5. **Trend Displays:** Historical & real-time X-Y plots.
> 6. **Alarm Summary Displays:** Chronological & priority-based alarm lists.

### Intuitive Analogy
A graphic display is to a plant operator what a **car's dashboard** is to a driver — instead of looking at the engine mechanically, the driver sees a speedometer, fuel gauge, and warning lights. Similarly, the operator does not inspect each pipe and tank physically; he views a graphical screen showing temperatures, pressures, and valve positions.

---

## 1.3 Panel Bid Specification

### Formal Definition
A **Panel Bid Specification** (or **Panel Specification Document**) is a formal engineering document issued by the client/consultant that defines **every technical, mechanical, electrical, and documentation requirement** for the design, fabrication, inspection, testing, and delivery of an Instrumentation Control Panel. Vendors use this document to prepare their **commercial and technical bids**.

### Key Sections of a Panel Bid Specification
- Scope of supply and exclusions
- Codes & standards (ISA, IEC, NEMA, IP rating)
- Mechanical construction (CRCA steel, thickness, painting, gland plate)
- Power distribution (MCB, MPCB, bus bars)
- Wiring, termination, and labelling
- Component approval list
- Inspection & testing (FAT/SAT)
- Documentation deliverables (datasheets, drawings, manuals)
- Warranty, training, and after-sales support

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks students to list sections of a panel specification or explain the difference between **Bid Specification** (client-issued) and **Bid Offer** (vendor response).

---

## 1.4 Safety — Introduction

### Formal Definition
**Industrial Safety** in instrumentation refers to the systematic application of engineering principles, regulations, and protective measures to **prevent injury to personnel, damage to equipment, and loss to the environment** during the design, installation, operation, and maintenance of process plants.

### Hierarchy of Safety Controls (In Order of Effectiveness)
1. **Elimination** — Physically remove the hazard.
2. **Substitution** — Replace with a less hazardous option.
3. **Engineering Controls** — Purging, pressurization, intrinsic safety.
4. **Administrative Controls** — Procedures, training, permits.
5. **PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)** — Helmets, gloves, arc-flash suits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 follows the **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511** framework for functional safety. Always cite the SIL (Safety Integrity Level) when discussing instrumented safety systems.

---

## 1.5 Electric Circuits Risk

### Formal Definition
**Electric Circuit Risk** refers to the potential for an electrical fault, malfunction, or human contact to cause **shock, burns, fire, explosion, or death**. In hazardous areas, electrical energy can ignite flammable atmospheres, while in normal areas, it can cause electrocution.

### Two Primary Categories of Risk
- **Risk to Personnel:** Electrocution, arc flash, secondary falls.
- **Risk to Process/Equipment:** Fire, explosion, toxic release, equipment damage.

### Intuitive Analogy
A live electrical circuit is like a **pressurized water hose with a hidden leak** — you cannot see the danger, but the moment you make contact (or in electrical terms, complete a path to ground), the energy discharges through you. Just as a leak detector is fitted to water systems, **safety devices (RCDs, fuses, IS barriers)** are fitted to electrical systems.

---

## 1.6 Flammable Atmosphere

### Formal Definition
A **Flammable Atmosphere** is a mixture of flammable gases, vapours, mists, or combustible dusts with air, in such proportions that it can ignite and cause an explosion when an ignition source of sufficient energy is present.

### The Fire Triangle (TETRAHEDRON)
The four elements required simultaneously:
1. **Fuel** (flammable gas/vapour)
2. **Oxidizer** (typically air/O₂ ≥ ~10% for most hydrocarbons)
3. **Ignition Source** (spark, hot surface, static)
4. **Unconfined Mixture** within flammable limits (LEL to UEL)

### Hazardous Area Classification (IEC 60079-10)
| Zone | Type | Frequency of Presence |
|------|------|------------------------|
| **Zone 0** | Gas/Vapour | Continuous or long periods (>1000 hrs/yr) |
| **Zone 1** | Gas/Vapour | Likely during normal operation (10–1000 hrs/yr) |
| **Zone 2** | Gas/Vapour | Not likely, short duration (<10 hrs/yr) |
| **Zone 20** | Dust | Continuous cloud |
| **Zone 21** | Dust | Likely during normal operation |
| **Zone 22** | Dust | Not likely, short duration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key:** Memorize that **Zone 0 requires intrinsic safety (Ex ia)**, **Zone 1 allows Ex d, Ex e, Ex p, Ex ib**, and **Zone 2 permits Ex nA (non-sparking) or any Zone 1 method.**

---

## 1.7 Electrocution & Let-Go Current

### Formal Definition
**Electrocution** is the physiological injury or death caused by electric current passing through the human body. The severity depends on **current magnitude, path, duration, and frequency**.

### Let-Go Current
**Let-go current** is the maximum current at which a person can still voluntarily release their grip on an energized conductor. Above this threshold, muscles contract involuntarily, and the victim cannot let go.

### Standard Thresholds (60 Hz AC, per IEC 60479 / NESC)
| Current (mA) | Physiological Effect |
|--------------|----------------------|
| **1 mA** | Threshold of perception, tingling |
| **5 mA** | Slight shock, not painful |
| **6–16 mA (men) / 9–15 mA (women)** | **LET-GO THRESHOLD** (muscle control lost) |
| **20–50 mA** | Painful shock, breathing difficult |
| **100–200 mA** | Ventricular fibrillation (often fatal) |
| **> 200 mA** | Severe burns, cardiac arrest, fatal |

> [!WARNING]
> **Dangerous Myth:** It is *voltage* that is often used in warnings, but it is *current* that kills. As low as **100 mA for 2 seconds** through the heart can cause fatal ventricular fibrillation. Ohm's Law (I = V/R) means that a body with wet skin (~1000 Ω) touching a 110 V supply already passes 110 mA — well above the lethal threshold.

---

## 1.8 Purging and Pressurization (Ex p)

### Formal Definition
**Purging and Pressurization** is an explosion protection technique where a **protective gas (usually clean air or inert gas)** is supplied into an electrical enclosure at a pressure slightly above the surrounding atmosphere, so that any flammable gas cannot enter the enclosure. This prevents the internal electrical components from coming into contact with a hazardous external atmosphere.

### Sequence
1. **Purging Phase:** Enclosure flushed with 5–10 enclosure volumes of clean air to remove any trapped gas.
2. **Pressurization Phase:** Maintain a positive pressure of ≥ 0.5 mbar (typical) above ambient.
3. **Operation Phase:** Continuous flow maintained; loss of pressure triggers an alarm or shutdown.

### Classification (NFPA 496 / IEC 60079-2)
- **Type X:** Reduces classification from Zone 1 to non-hazardous.
- **Type Y:** Reduces from Zone 1 to Zone 2.
- **Type Z:** Reduces from Zone 2 to non-hazardous.

---

## 1.9 Intrinsic Safety (IS)

### Formal Definition
**Intrinsic Safety (IS)** is an explosion protection technique in which the **electrical energy within the circuit is restricted to a level below that which could ignite a flammable atmosphere**, even under fault conditions. It is the only technique that is safe even when the equipment is open or during maintenance.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of intrinsic safety like **giving a child a battery-powered toy** instead of a real tool — even if the child misuses it, the toy cannot cause serious harm because the energy is inherently limited. Similarly, an IS circuit cannot spark hard enough to ignite gas, no matter what fault occurs.

### Two Types
- **Ex ib:** Safe with **one fault** (used in Zone 1).
- **Ex ia:** Safe with **two faults** (used in Zone 0).

---

## 1.10 Safety Instrumented System (SIS) & Process Safety

### Formal Definition
A **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** is an engineered system composed of **sensors, logic solvers, and final control elements** that independently monitors the process and takes the plant to a safe state when pre-determined unsafe conditions are detected. It is distinct from the Basic Process Control System (BPCS).

### Safety Integrity Levels (SIL) — Per IEC 61508
| SIL | PFD (Prob. of Failure on Demand) | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) |
|-----|----------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **SIL 1** | 10⁻² to 10⁻¹ | 10–100 |
| **SIL 2** | 10⁻³ to 10⁻² | 100–1000 |
| **SIL 3** | 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³ | 1000–10000 |
| **SIL 4** | 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁴ | 10000–100000 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024:** Most petrochemical and process industries operate at **SIL 2 or SIL 3** for critical loops (e.g., burner management, HIPPS, ESD).

### Intuitive Analogy
The BPCS is the **driver of the car** (controls speed and direction). The SIS is the **automatic emergency braking system + airbags** — it does not drive the car; it only acts when something goes wrong.

---

## 1.11 Intrinsic Safety Barrier

### Formal Definition
An **Intrinsic Safety Barrier** is a network of **Zener diodes, resistors, and fuses** placed in series and parallel within a safety barrier device. It is installed at the boundary between the **safe area (non-hazardous)** and the **hazardous area** to limit the voltage and current that can flow into the hazardous zone under normal and fault conditions.

### Two Main Types
- **Zener Barrier:** Uses Zener diodes to clamp voltage; requires a high-integrity earth connection.
- **Galvanic Isolator (Isolated Barrier):** Uses transformers and opto-couplers; provides galvanic isolation, so no dedicated earth is required.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Air Header — Design Logic & Engineering Analysis

### Operational Steps
1. **Air Intake:** Atmospheric air is drawn in through a filter.
2. **Compression:** A multi-stage compressor raises pressure to **7 bar (typical)**.
3. **Drying & Filtration:** Removes moisture, oil, and particulates.
4. **Storage:** Receiver tank smooths pulsations.
5. **Distribution:** Air header receives stabilized air and feeds branches.
6. **Pressure Regulation:** Branch regulators drop pressure to instrument requirement (**1.4 bar** for typical pneumatic controllers).
7. **Isolation:** Each branch has a manual ball valve + double-block-and-bleed for maintenance.

### Why an Air Header is Critical
- A **single header failure** can disable hundreds of instruments.
- Pressure fluctuations cause **setpoint drift** in controllers.
- Moisture ingress leads to **freeze-up** in cold climates and **sticking valves**.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
Used in refineries, petrochemical plants, fertilizer plants, paper mills, and pharmaceutical plants — anywhere **pneumatic instrumentation (legacy or modern I/P converters)** is deployed.

---

## 2.2 Graphic Display — Hierarchy of HMI Screens (Per ISA 101.01)

### Screen Pyramid (Bottom = Plant-Wide, Top = Detail)
1. **Plant Overview Screen** — Plant-wide KPIs and major unit status.
2. **Area / Unit Control Screen** — Process flow diagram (P&ID derived) of a unit.
3. **Group Control Screen** — Section-wise detail (e.g., distillation column top).
4. **Loop / Detail Screen** — Single PID faceplate with tuning.
5. **Trend Screen** — Historical and real-time trends.
6. **Alarm Screen** — Active, acknowledged, and historical alarms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024:** ISA 101.01 mandates **High-Performance HMI (HP HMI)** philosophy: use **mimic-based**, **grey-scale**, **task-oriented** screens rather than colourful 3D graphics, to reduce operator cognitive load.

---

## 2.3 Panel Bid Specification — Deep Sections

### Mechanical Construction
- **Material:** 2 mm CRCA steel (or 14 SWG).
- **Painting:** Powder-coated RAL 7035 (light grey) for inside, RAL 5012 (blue) for outside.
- **Ingress Protection:** **IP 54** for indoor, **IP 55/65** for outdoor.
- **Cable Entry:** Bottom through gland plate; **20% spare glands** mandatory.
- **Doors:** Hinged with concealed hinges; rubber gaskets.

### Electrical Construction
- **Main Power:** 110 V AC or 230 V AC, 50 Hz; redundant feeders.
- **Bus Bars:** Tinned copper, sized for 1.25× full load current.
- **MCB/MPCB:** Selective coordination.
- **Earthing:** Two independent earth pits (<1 Ω each).
- **Internal Wiring:** 1.5 mm² stranded copper, **ferrule-numbered**.
- **Signal Wiring:** Shielded twisted pair, single-end grounded.

---

## 2.4 Electrical Safety Analysis — Ohm's Law Applied to Human Body

### Formula Derivation Chain

Ohm's Law for the human body:

$$
I_{body} = \frac{V_{source}}{Z_{body}}
$$

Where:
- $V_{source}$ = Source voltage (V)
- $Z_{body}$ = Body impedance (Ω) — typically 1000 Ω dry, 300 Ω wet
- $I_{body}$ = Current through body (A)

**Critical Threshold:** Ventricular Fibrillation threshold is approximately:

$$
I_{fibrillation} \approx \frac{K}{\sqrt{t}}
$$

Where:
- $K$ = Empirical constant (≈ 116 for hand-to-hand path, 50/60 Hz)
- $t$ = Duration of exposure (seconds)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Calculation:** A 230 V AC source contacts a person with **wet skin (Z = 1500 Ω)**. Calculate the current.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units | Used For |
|---------|-------------|-------|----------|
| $I = \frac{V}{Z}$ | Ohm's Law for body current | A (V, Ω) | Shock severity |
| $I_{fibrillation} = \frac{116}{\sqrt{t}}$ | IEC 60479 fibrillation threshold | mA (s) | AC 50/60 Hz, body path |
| $PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_i}{2}$ | Average Probability of Failure on Demand | dimensionless | SIL verification |
| $RRF = \frac{1}{PFD}$ | Risk Reduction Factor | dimensionless | SIS assessment |
| $MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time To Failure | hours | Reliability |
| $\lambda = \lambda_D + \lambda_S$ | Total failure rate = dangerous + safe | /hr | Reliability block |
| $DC = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_D}$ | Diagnostic Coverage | 0 to 1 | SIF architecture |
| $PFH = \lambda_D \cdot (1-DC) \cdot t_{CE}$ | Probability of Failure per Hour | /hr | SIL 3/4 verification |
| $Q = C_v \cdot N \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{SG}}$ | Air header flow capacity | Nm³/h | Header sizing |
| $P_{relief} = 1.1 \times P_{design}$ | Safety relief valve set pressure | bar | Air receiver |
| $V_{purge} = 5 \times V_{enclosure}$ | Purge volume requirement | m³ | Ex p design |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table syntax safeguard:** All absolute values above use plain notation (e.g., $P_{design}$) to avoid breaking the markdown table. In LaTeX, use `\vert` or `\mid` for magnitude bars.

---

## 2.6 Intrinsic Safety — Energy Limitation Principle

The IS barrier limits three parameters:
- **Voltage (V):** Limited to ≤ **24 V** typically (for hydrogen groups).
- **Current (I):** Limited by series resistor.
- **Power (P):** Limited to ≤ **1.3 W** for Group IIC (hydrogen).

The energy stored in any inductance (L) or capacitance (C) is also limited:

$$
E_L = \frac{1}{2} L I^2 \leq E_{min}
$$

$$
E_C = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 \leq E_{min}
$$

Where $E_{min}$ is the **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE)** of the gas (hydrogen ≈ 20 μJ).

---

## 2.7 Safety Instrumented System — Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)

### Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)
1. **Process Design** (inherent safety)
2. **BPCS — Basic Control**
3. **Alarms & Operator Intervention**
4. **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)**
5. **Physical Relief (PSV, rupture disc)**
6. **Plant Emergency Response**
7. **Community Emergency Response**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024:** When LOPA is performed, the target is to reduce the **Tolerable Risk** to within the **ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)** region.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Current Through Human Body (Electrocution Analysis)

### Problem Statement
A worker in a process plant touches a **230 V AC, 50 Hz** live terminal with **wet hands (Z_body = 1500 Ω)**. Determine:
(a) The current flowing through the body.
(b) Compare with the let-go threshold.
(c) State the physiological effect.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the body impedance and source voltage.

$$
V_{source} = 230 \text{ V (AC)}
$$

$$
Z_{body} = 1500 \text{ Ω (wet skin condition)}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Ohm's Law to compute the body current.

$$
I_{body} = \frac{V_{source}}{Z_{body}}
$$

$$
I_{body} = \frac{230}{1500}
$$

$$
I_{body} = 0.1533 \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_{body} = 153.3 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with the let-go threshold (16 mA for men, 15 mA for women).

The let-go threshold is the maximum current at which voluntary muscle control is retained:

$$
I_{let\text{-}go, men} = 16 \text{ mA}
$$

$$
I_{let\text{-}go, women} = 15 \text{ mA}
$$

The calculated body current is:

$$
I_{body} = 153.3 \text{ mA} \gg 16 \text{ mA}
$$

So the let-go threshold is exceeded by a factor of approximately:

$$
\text{Factor} = \frac{153.3}{16} \approx 9.58
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the IEC fibrillation formula for verification.

$$
I_{fibrillation} = \frac{K}{\sqrt{t}}
$$

For a typical shock duration of $t = 1$ second and $K = 116$ (hand-to-hand path):

$$
I_{fibrillation} = \frac{116}{\sqrt{1}} = 116 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 5:** Compare with body current.

$$
I_{body} = 153.3 \text{ mA} > I_{fibrillation} = 116 \text{ mA}
$$

Therefore, **ventricular fibrillation is virtually certain**.

**Step 6:** Conclusion.

The physiological effect is **severe: ventricular fibrillation, possible cardiac arrest, severe burns, and probable death within 2–4 minutes if first aid is not administered.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Air Header Sizing for Branch Flow

### Problem Statement
An air header must supply **30 pneumatic instruments**, each consuming **0.5 Nm³/h** at 6 bar. The header length is 50 m. Compute the total flow demand and the minimum header diameter (assuming an allowable pressure drop of 0.2 bar and air velocity of 25 m/s).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute total flow demand.

$$
Q_{total} = N \times Q_{instrument}
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 30 \times 0.5 = 15 \text{ Nm}^3\text{/h}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert flow to volumetric flow at line conditions (6 bar abs, 25°C).

Line density at 6 bar:

$$
\rho_{line} = \rho_{atm} \times \frac{P_{line}}{P_{atm}} = 1.2 \times \frac{6}{1} = 7.2 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

Volumetric flow at line:

$$
Q_{line} = \frac{Q_{total} \times \rho_{atm}}{3600 \times \rho_{line}}
$$

$$
Q_{line} = \frac{15 \times 1.2}{3600 \times 7.2} = 6.94 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3\text{/s}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute minimum cross-sectional area for the allowed velocity.

$$
A_{min} = \frac{Q_{line}}{v} = \frac{6.94 \times 10^{-4}}{25}
$$

$$
A_{min} = 2.78 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the minimum diameter.

$$
D_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times A_{min}}{\pi}}
$$

$$
D_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 2.78 \times 10^{-5}}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{3.54 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
D_{min} = 5.95 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 5.95 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply a safety factor of 2× and round up to a standard size.

$$
D_{design} = 2 \times 5.95 = 11.9 \text{ mm}
$$

**Conclusion:** Use a **standard 1/2" (12.7 mm) header** for this branch, or a **2" (50 mm) main header** for the main distribution line per industry convention.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: SIL Verification Using PFD

### Problem Statement
A Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) has the following parameters:
- Dangerous failure rate: $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour
- Proof test interval: $T_i = 8760$ hours (1 year)
- Diagnostic coverage: $DC = 0.9$ (90%)

Determine the SIL level achieved.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** For a 1oo1 (one-out-of-one) architecture, average PFD is:

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_D \times T_i}{2}
$$

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760}{2}
$$

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{0.0438}{2} = 0.0219
$$

**Step 2:** Apply diagnostic coverage (only undetected dangerous failures count).

$$
PFD_{avg, eff} = PFD_{avg} \times (1 - DC)
$$

$$
PFD_{avg, eff} = 0.0219 \times (1 - 0.9) = 0.0219 \times 0.1
$$

$$
PFD_{avg, eff} = 0.00219 = 2.19 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with SIL ranges.

$$
\text{SIL 2 range: } 10^{-3} \leq PFD < 10^{-2}
$$

$$
\text{SIL 3 range: } 10^{-4} \leq PFD < 10^{-3}
$$

Since $PFD = 2.19 \times 10^{-3}$:

$$
2.19 \times 10^{-3} \geq 10^{-3} \text{ and } < 10^{-2}
$$

**Conclusion:** The SIF achieves **SIL 2**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Note:** To upgrade to SIL 3, either reduce $T_i$ to ~6 months ($T_i = 4380$ hr) OR increase diagnostic coverage to 99% (DC = 0.99).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Safety Method Selection by Zone

| Hazard Type | Zone | Method Allowed | Notation |
|-------------|------|----------------|----------|
| Gas/Vapour | **Zone 0** | Intrinsic Safety (ia), Encapsulation (ma) | Ex ia, Ex ma |
| Gas/Vapour | **Zone 1** | Flameproof (d), Increased Safety (e), Purged (p), IS (ib), Oil Immersion (o), Powder Filling (q) | Ex d, Ex e, Ex p, Ex ib |
| Gas/Vapour | **Zone 2** | Non-Sparking (nA), Restricted Breathing (nR), or any Zone 1 method | Ex nA, Ex nR |
| Dust | **Zone 20** | Intrinsic Safety (iaD), Encapsulation (maD) | Ex iaD |
| Dust | **Zone 21** | Protection by enclosure (tD) | Ex tD |
| Dust | **Zone 22** | Protection by enclosure or any Zone 21 method | Ex tD |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **"increased safety (Ex e)"** with **"intrinsic safety (Ex i)"**. Ex e is *not* intrinsically safe — it merely increases the safety margin (creepage, clearance, temperature) so that sparks are unlikely under normal operation.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: PFD vs SIL Cross-Check (IEC 61508)

| Architecture | PFD Formula | Notes |
|--------------|-------------|-------|
| 1oo1 | $\frac{\lambda_D T_i}{2}$ | Simplest; no redundancy |
| 1oo2 | $\frac{(\lambda_D T_i)^2}{3}$ | High reliability; fails only if both fail |
| 2oo2 | $2 \times (1 - e^{-\lambda_D T_i})$ | Both must work; used for high-demand |
| 2oo3 | $\frac{(\lambda_D T_i)^2}{3} \times \text{vote logic}$ | Voting for spurious trip reduction |

> [!NOTE]
> **1oo1** = "1 out of 1" = single channel. **2oo3** = "2 out of 3" = three channels, any two must trip.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Q: Define Intrinsic Safety. Why is it the preferred protection method for Zone 0 hazardous areas?**

**Model Answer:**
Intrinsic Safety (IS) is an explosion protection technique that limits the electrical energy (voltage, current, and power) within a circuit to a level below the Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE) of the surrounding flammable atmosphere, even under fault conditions.

It is preferred for **Zone 0** because:
1. Zone 0 has a continuous or long-duration presence of a flammable atmosphere.
2. Other methods (Ex d, Ex p) assume a flammable gas is *not* present inside the enclosure; in Zone 0, this assumption fails.
3. IS is **inherently safe** — even if the equipment is opened for live maintenance, it cannot ignite the gas.
4. IS requires only **Ex ia** certification, which is safe under **two simultaneous faults** — the highest safety level.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "IS means no sparks can occur." This is **incorrect** — IS limits energy such that any spark that occurs is **below the MIE of the gas**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Q: What is the "Let-Go Current"? State its typical value for men and women at 50 Hz AC.**

**Model Answer:**
Let-Go current is the **maximum current** through the human body at which a person can still voluntarily release his/her grip on an energized conductor. Beyond this value, the muscles contract involuntarily, and the victim cannot let go, leading to prolonged exposure and death.

Typical values at 50 Hz AC (per IEC 60479):
- **Men:** 16 mA
- **Women:** 15 mA

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Some students confuse "Let-Go" with "perception" threshold (1 mA). Let-Go is a **muscle-control** threshold, not a sensation threshold.

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the construction and design of an **Air Header** for a process plant. Discuss air quality requirements, sizing principles, and safety considerations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a detailed technical note on **Graphic Displays** used in modern control rooms. Explain the HMI hierarchy per ISA 101.01, with a note on High-Performance HMI philosophy. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Air Header

**1. Definition:** An air header is a metallic manifold that distributes clean, dry, oil-free instrument air from the receiver tank to multiple branches serving pneumatic instruments.

**2. Construction:**
- Material: Carbon steel (ASTM A106) or stainless steel (SS 304/316).
- Diameter: 2" to 6" main header; 1/2" or 1/4" branch connections.
- Fittings: Welded joints on main, threaded NPT on branches.
- Mounting: Floor-stand or wall-mount with saddles.

**3. Components on a typical header:**
- **Pressure Gauge:** 0–10 bar, dual-scale.
- **Safety Relief Valve:** Set at 1.1 × working pressure.
- **Isolation Ball Valves:** One per branch, full-bore.
- **Drain Valve:** At the lowest point for condensate removal.
- **Pressure Regulator:** Branch-wise drop to 1.4 bar (for pneumatic controllers).
- **Pressure Switch:** Low-pressure alarm at 4.0 bar.

**4. Air Quality (ISO 8573-1 Class 2):**
- Particle size ≤ 1 μm
- Pressure dew point ≤ -40°C
- Oil content ≤ 0.1 mg/m³

**5. Sizing Principle:**
The header diameter is calculated using the **velocity method** (typically 25–30 m/s) or the **pressure drop method** (max 0.2 bar drop across the header).

$$
D_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{4 Q_{line}}{\pi v}}
$$

**6. Safety Considerations:**
- Redundant air compressors (1 working + 1 standby).
- Backup nitrogen connection for instrument air continuity.
- Surge protection upstream of header.
- Routine drain of moisture traps.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Graphic Displays

**1. Definition:** A graphic display is a software-based HMI screen that visualizes the process in real time.

**2. ISA 101.01 Hierarchy (5 levels):**
- **Level 1:** Plant Overview (KPIs).
- **Level 2:** Area/Unit Control (P&ID mimic).
- **Level 3:** Group Control (sub-system detail).
- **Level 4:** Loop/Detail (PID faceplate, tuning).
- **Level 5:** Trend and Alarm (historical).

**3. High-Performance HMI Philosophy:**
- **Mimic-based**, not 3D or photographic.
- **Grey-scale** with selective use of colour (e.g., only deviation/quality).
- **Task-oriented** — operator sees only what is needed for current task.
- **Reduced alarm flood** — alarm rationalization per ISA 18.2.
- **Consistent navigation** — same button positions on every screen.

**4. Conclusion:** Modern graphic displays reduce operator cognitive load and improve plant safety.

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** Discuss **Electrical Safety** in hazardous areas. Explain **Purging and Pressurization** as a protection technique, with the three types per NFPA 496. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the architecture and installation of an **Intrinsic Safety Barrier**. Draw a typical Zener barrier circuit and describe its operation. Provide **two industrial examples** of IS barrier application. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Electrical Safety & Purging/Pressurization

**1. Electrical Hazards in Hazardous Areas:**
- Ignition of flammable gas by spark or hot surface.
- Electric shock to personnel (electrocution).
- Arc flash causing burns and blast pressure.
- Static discharge igniting dust clouds.

**2. Protection Techniques (Ex d, Ex e, Ex p, Ex i, Ex o, Ex q, Ex m, Ex n):**
- Ex d: Flameproof (contains internal explosion).
- Ex e: Increased safety (prevention of sparks).
- Ex p: Purging and pressurization (positive pressure).
- Ex i: Intrinsic safety (energy limitation).

**3. Purging and Pressurization (Ex p):**
This technique supplies clean air (or inert gas) into an enclosure to:
- Remove any flammable gas that may have entered.
- Maintain positive pressure to prevent ingress.

**4. Sequence of Operation:**
- **Step 1:** Pre-purge — Enclosure is purged with 5–10 volumes of clean air.
- **Step 2:** Pressurization — Pressure is maintained ≥ 0.5 mbar above ambient.
- **Step 3:** Operation — Continuous flow with monitoring.
- **Step 4:** Failure — Loss of pressure triggers alarm and de-energization.

**5. NFPA 496 / IEC 60079-2 Types:**
| Type | Reduces From | To |
|------|---------------|-----|
| **Type X** | Zone 1 | Non-hazardous |
| **Type Y** | Zone 1 | Zone 2 |
| **Type Z** | Zone 2 | Non-hazardous |

**6. Conclusion:** Ex p is suitable for large enclosures (e.g., analyzer shelters, control panels) in Zone 1 areas.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — IS Barrier Architecture & Examples

**1. Definition:** An IS barrier is a network of Zener diodes, resistors, and fuses that limits voltage, current, and power entering a hazardous area.

**2. Internal Circuit Description:**
- **Fuse (F1):** Rated 50 mA; protects against sustained over-current.
- **Resistor (R1):** 250 Ω; limits current to safe value.
- **Zener Diodes (Z1, Z2):** Two in parallel (redundancy); clamp voltage to ≤ 24 V.
- **Earth Terminal:** Provides a return path for Zener current; must be < 1 Ω.

**3. Operation:**
Under **normal conditions**, signals pass through the barrier with minimal loss. Under a **fault** (e.g., mains voltage accidentally applied to the safe-area terminal), the Zeners conduct the excess current to earth, blowing the fuse and isolating the hazardous area.

**4. Zener Barrier vs. Galvanic Isolator:**
| Feature | Zener Barrier | Galvanic Isolator |
|---------|---------------|------------------|
| Earth Required | **Yes** | **No** |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Isolation | None (common earth) | Full galvanic isolation |
| Maintenance | Earth integrity critical | Simple |

**5. Two Industrial Examples:**

**Example 1 — Hydrocarbon Storage Tank Farm (Zone 0/1):**
A radar level transmitter (VEGA, E+H) installed on a crude oil storage tank (Zone 0 inside, Zone 1 around the manhole) is connected to the DCS via a Zener barrier mounted in the safe-area marshalling cabinet. The barrier limits energy to 24 V / 20 mA, ensuring safety even during tank cleaning with hydrocarbon vapours present.

**Example 2 — Hydrogen Compressor House (Zone 1, Group IIC):**
A pressure transmitter (Rosemount 3051) on a hydrogen compressor is connected via a galvanic isolator (Phoenix Contact MACX MCR) to the BPCS. Because the system is Group IIC (hydrogen — most easily ignited gas), Ex ia IIC certification is mandatory. The isolator provides 3-port isolation (input/output/power) and eliminates earth loops.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Purging with Pressurization** — they are not the same. Purging is the *initial flush*; pressurization is the *maintained state*.
> 2. **Missing Earth Connection** in Zener barrier diagrams — earth is mandatory; examiners specifically check for it.
> 3. **Wrong Zone Classification** — students assign Ex d to Zone 0 (incorrect; only Ex ia allowed).
> 4. **Forgetting to mention** "minimum two faults" for Ex ia — examiners award marks for this specific phrase.
> 5. **Let-Go current unit confusion** — must be in **mA**, not A.
> 6. **In PFD calculations**, forgetting to multiply by **(1 − DC)** — a common error worth 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Confusing SIL with SIF** — SIL is the *level*; SIF is the *function*.

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4:**

### Air Header
- Working pressure **6 bar**, branch pressure **1.4 bar**.
- Material: CS or SS, fitted with **safety relief valve, pressure gauge, drain valve, isolation valve**.
- Air quality per **ISO 8573-1 Class 2** (dew point -40°C, 1 μm, 0.1 mg/m³ oil).

### Graphic Display
- **ISA 101.01** HMI hierarchy: Plant → Area → Group → Loop → Trend/Alarm.
- High-Performance HMI = mimic + grey-scale + task-oriented.

### Panel Bid Specification
- **Mechanical:** 2 mm CRCA, RAL 7035 inside, RAL 5012 outside, IP 54 indoor.
- **Electrical:** 110/230 V AC, 50 Hz, two earth pits (<1 Ω), 1.5 mm² wires, ferrule-numbered.
- **Documentation:** As-built drawings, FAT/SAT reports, O&M manuals, calibration certificates.

### Safety Fundamentals
- Hierarchy: Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- Fire triangle: Fuel + Air + Ignition → Add confinement for explosion.

### Electric Circuit Risk
- It is **current that kills, not voltage**.
- Let-Go: **16 mA (men), 15 mA (women)** at 50 Hz AC.
- Fibrillation threshold: $I = \frac{116}{\sqrt{t}}$ mA.

### Flammable Atmosphere
- Zones: **0, 1, 2** for gas; **20, 21, 22** for dust.
- Zone 0 → only **Ex ia**.
- Zone 1 → Ex d, Ex e, Ex p, Ex ib, Ex o, Ex q, Ex m.
- Zone 2 → Ex nA, Ex nR, or any Zone 1 method.

### Purging & Pressurization (Ex p)
- **Type X:** Zone 1 → Safe.
- **Type Y:** Zone 1 → Zone 2.
- **Type Z:** Zone 2 → Safe.
- Purging volume: **5 to 10 × enclosure volume** of clean air.

### Intrinsic Safety
- **Ex ib:** 1 fault (Zone 1).
- **Ex ia:** 2 faults (Zone 0).
- Limits: V ≤ 24 V, I ≤ 20 mA, P ≤ 1.3 W (Group IIC hydrogen).
- MIE of hydrogen ≈ **20 μJ**.

### IS Barrier
- **Zener barrier:** Fuses + resistors + Zener diodes; **requires dedicated earth**.
- **Galvanic isolator:** Transformer + opto-coupler; **no earth required**.

### SIS & Process Safety
- SIS is **independent** of BPCS (separate sensors, logic, final elements).
- **SIL 1:** PFD 10⁻² to 10⁻¹; **SIL 2:** 10⁻³ to 10⁻²; **SIL 3:** 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³; **SIL 4:** 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁴.
- PFD formula for 1oo1: $\frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_i}{2}$.
- Standard followed: **IEC 61508** (general) and **IEC 61511** (process industry).
- Architecture: 1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for Hazardous Zones:**
> **"012 for Gas, 202122 for Dust"** — Zones get higher numbers for less frequent presence. Memorize: **Zone 0 = continuous**, **Zone 1 = likely**, **Zone 2 = unlikely**.

> [!TIP]
> **SIL Mnemonic:** **"1 2 3 4 — Powers of 10"** — Each SIL improves PFD by one order of magnitude (10⁻¹, 10⁻², 10⁻³, 10⁻⁴).
